scenario: A dashboard sidebar filtering projects by search text.
submitter_context: PR: 'Add search to project sidebar. Used useMemo for the filtered list. Profiled locally with 100 items and feels snappy.'
looks_reasonable_because: Passes local testing with small lists
review_summary: Will jank on every keystroke once the list passes ~500 items. useMemo memoizes against a dep that changes per keystroke, .sort mutates the projects prop, and the inline onClick defeats child memo. Three orthogonal perf bugs that only show up with prod data.

issues_found.title: .sort() mutates the projects prop
issues_found.explanation: Array.prototype.sort mutates in place. .filter shields it today, but the moment someone refactors to sort-then-filter, or projects comes from a Zustand store, you get phantom reorders bypassing change detection. Old footgun.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating .sort() as pure. It returns the array but mutates it.
issues_found.fix: Use .toSorted() (ES2023) or spread before sorting.
issues_found.verification: Pass Object.freeze(projects) — should not throw.

issues_found.title: useMemo recomputes every keystroke
issues_found.explanation: query changes per keypress, invalidating the memo every time. At 5000 items the filter+sort runs synchronously on the input handler hot path. 'react' = 100ms of jank. useMemo is a comfort blanket here, not optimization.
issues_found.root_cause: Confusing memoization with optimization. Memoization needs stable inputs.
issues_found.fix: Use useDeferredValue on query. Filter against deferredQuery — typing stays 60fps even when filtering is slow.
issues_found.verification: Profile with 5000 items. Keystroke commits <8ms.

issues_found.title: Inline arrow function breaks child memoization
issues_found.explanation: New function reference every render defeats React.memo. 1000 rows means 1000 row re-renders per keystroke for no reason.
issues_found.root_cause: Arrow functions in JSX create new refs each render; React.memo compares by reference.
issues_found.fix: useCallback at the parent passing id, or move handler into ProjectRow with onSelect.
issues_found.verification: Wrap ProjectRow in React.memo + console.log in body; type — only matching rows re-render.

issues_found.title: No virtualization for unbounded list
issues_found.explanation: 5000 rows = 5000 DOM nodes, 800ms+ initial render. overflow-y-auto is the tell the author knew it could be long.
issues_found.root_cause: Assuming browsers handle long lists. They don't.
issues_found.fix: @tanstack/react-virtual.
issues_found.verification: 10k items <100ms paint, 60fps scroll.

issues_found.title: Loop-invariant computation in hot loop
issues_found.explanation: toLowerCase per-item, 4999 redundant allocations on 5k items.
issues_found.root_cause: Loop-invariant inside a loop.
issues_found.fix: Hoist: const q = query.toLowerCase().
issues_found.verification: Benchmark — ~2x faster.

issues_found.title: Missing aria-pressed + visible active state
issues_found.explanation: Screen reader can't tell which sort is active; sighted users can't either.
issues_found.root_cause: Buttons treated as actions not toggles.
issues_found.fix: aria-pressed={sortBy==='name'} + aria-pressed:bg-neutral-200 in Tailwind v4.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces 'pressed' on active.

issues_that_arent_issues: button without type='button' outside a form is fine. h-screen on aside is intentional. console.log is obvious placeholder.
broader_pattern: useMemo/React.memo aren't free 'make-it-fast' buttons. They only help with stable inputs over expensive work. For input-driven recomputation reach for useDeferredValue, virtualization, and debouncing — and verify with the Profiler.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Calling out useMemo as theater when the dep changes per keystroke comes from debugging junior PRs that pasted useMemo everywhere yet still shipped janky UIs. .sort mutation is dormant here but a senior flags the future refactor. Virtualization is a judgment call — overflow-y-auto plus a search input is a tell the author expected scale and didn't plan for it.

scenario: useWebSocket hook: open a connection, expose latest message and a send function.
submitter_context: PR: 'Add useWebSocket for live updates. Cleans up on unmount. Tested on staging.'
review_summary: Three landmines. ws.close() is async — Strict Mode double-effect creates two sockets briefly, both delivering messages. JSON.parse crashes on heartbeats. send() throws InvalidStateError if called before OPEN. And the cast 'as T' is runtime fiction.

issues_found.title: Race: new socket opens before old one closes
issues_found.explanation: WebSocket.close() initiates an async handshake. In Strict Mode dev (and on url change), cleanup runs but close doesn't finish before next effect creates a new ws. Two sockets, both with handlers, both calling setMessage. In prod with server-side reconnect logic you get duplicate/out-of-order updates intermittently — devs dismiss as 'Strict Mode being weird.'
issues_found.root_cause: Treating WS close as synchronous and not guarding handlers against stale sockets.
issues_found.fix: Cancellation flag in closure; guard every handler. Null out handlers in cleanup. ws.close(1000, 'unmount').
issues_found.verification: Strict Mode dev — log a connection id per event; only latest connection should hit state.

issues_found.title: JSON.parse can crash the handler
issues_found.explanation: Servers send heartbeats, plaintext errors, partial frames. JSON.parse('PING\n') throws synchronously inside the handler. Not in render, so no error boundary catches it. Silent console error; the next real message never arrives because the connection gets into a wedged state.
issues_found.root_cause: Trusting the wire format. WS is byte transport, not JSON.
issues_found.fix: try/catch around parse. Log truncated raw on failure.
issues_found.verification: Send non-JSON frame; should log warning, not crash.

issues_found.title: send() throws if called before OPEN or after CLOSING
issues_found.explanation: Optional chaining handles null but WebSocket in CONNECTING(1) or CLOSING(2) state still throws InvalidStateError on send. User clicking send before connect = JS exception, no UI feedback.
issues_found.root_cause: Optional chaining handles null, not invalid object state.
issues_found.fix: Check readyState === OPEN before send. Return boolean.
issues_found.verification: send() in mount effect before WS connects — should not throw.

issues_found.title: Only latest message retained — data loss under burst
issues_found.explanation: setMessage overwrites; React batches setState, last write wins. Two rapid messages in the same task = first dropped. For chat or telemetry that's data loss.
issues_found.root_cause: Designing API around a single render rather than stream semantics.
issues_found.fix: Rename to useLatestMessage, or expose onMessage callback for stream.
issues_found.verification: Send 5 rapid messages; consumer's handler must receive all 5.

issues_found.title: Runtime has no idea what came over the wire
issues_found.explanation: as T is 'trust me' — autocomplete works, runtime crashes when backend changes schema.
issues_found.root_cause: Type assertion where runtime validation is required.
issues_found.fix: Accept a Zod schema option. Parse failure -> onError.
issues_found.verification: Pass non-matching schema — onError fires, no bad T.

issues_found.title: Missing error and reconnecting states
issues_found.explanation: Two-state model can't represent 'connection failed, retrying.' Consumers reinvent it.
issues_found.root_cause: Modeling only the happy path.
issues_found.fix: Add 'error' (and optionally 'reconnecting') states. Expose lastError.
issues_found.verification: Drop network — status should reflect error.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using onmessage= over addEventListener is fine for a single owner. Not using useSyncExternalStore is acceptable since we want React state semantics.
broader_pattern: Network hooks must treat every async boundary as a place where the component can unmount, URL can change, or message can be malformed. Build cancellation tokens, validate at boundaries, never assume a clean lifecycle from external systems.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The cancellation-token pattern in async effects isn't in most tutorials — you learn it after debugging Strict Mode double-fire issues that ship as duplicate API calls. JSON.parse-crashes-handler survives unit tests (always valid JSON) and explodes when a load balancer injects probes. Insisting on runtime validation despite generic T is experience of shipping 'works on my machine' types that explode when a backend team adds an enum value.

scenario: Search results that fetch when query changes. Should show latest query's results.
submitter_context: PR: 'Fetch search results on query change. Loading state. async/await for readability.'
review_summary: Classic stale-response race: type 'react' fast, 'r' resolves after 'react', user sees 'r' results under a 'react' input. Plus loading flicker, no error handling, no debounce, no empty state. Ships constantly.

issues_found.title: Stale response wins the race
issues_found.explanation: Requests don't return in issue order. A (slow) for 'r' resolves after B (fast) for 'react' — UI shows wrong results. Intermittent, network-jitter-dependent, never reproduces in QA.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating fetch as if responses arrive in request order.
issues_found.fix: AbortController; abort previous on cleanup. Or use TanStack Query / React 19 use() with Suspense.
issues_found.verification: Throttle to 3G, type fast, displayed results must match current query.

issues_found.title: No error handling — stuck on 'Loading...'
issues_found.explanation: fetch rejects on network drop; await throws; setLoading(false) never runs. Spinner forever, refresh blamed on the app.
issues_found.root_cause: Async fn in useEffect with no try/catch.
issues_found.fix: try/catch/finally, surface error state.
issues_found.verification: Kill server mid-typing; should show error, not hang.

issues_found.title: No response.ok check
issues_found.explanation: 500 returns parse as JSON anyway; data.results undefined; .map crashes. Or auth redirect returns HTML, res.json() throws SyntaxError attributed to wrong code.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating fetch like axios — fetch doesn't reject on HTTP errors.
issues_found.fix: Check res.ok and content-type before json().
issues_found.verification: Server returns 500 — should show error, not crash.

issues_found.title: Loading flicker on fast searches
issues_found.explanation: Each keystroke toggles loading; on fast servers the spinner flashes per keystroke, more distracting than useful.
issues_found.root_cause: Naive boolean loading for a streaming sequence of queries.
issues_found.fix: useTransition's isPending + useDeferredValue, or show spinner after 200ms delay.
issues_found.verification: Type fast — no flicker.

issues_found.title: No debouncing — request per character
issues_found.explanation: 'react' = 5 requests. Rate limits, cost. Even with cancellation, the requests start.
issues_found.root_cause: Confusing 'effect runs on change' with 'effect should act on every change.'
issues_found.fix: useDeferredValue or debounce 200-300ms.
issues_found.verification: Network tab — ~1 request per word, not per char.

issues_found.title: No empty state
issues_found.explanation: Empty array renders nothing — indistinguishable from broken or loading.
issues_found.root_cause: List UI without explicit empty state.
issues_found.fix: Show 'No results for X' when not loading and results is empty.
issues_found.verification: Search gibberish — show 'No results.'

issues_that_arent_issues: encodeURIComponent correct. Semantic <ul>/<li> good. The query.length>1 short-circuit is fine policy.
broader_pattern: Every async effect that touches state must answer: (1) what if a newer one started? (2) what if this one fails? (3) what if the component unmounted? If you can't answer all three you have a race condition shipping. Better: don't write data-fetching effects — use a query library or Suspense.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The race condition is textbook but spotting loading flicker as UX-wrong despite being logically correct is judgment. So is recommending TanStack Query — 'don't write this hook' is often the right comment but takes seniority to say diplomatically.

scenario: Generic Table component with column defs, sortable, strongly-typed rows.
submitter_context: PR: 'Reusable Table<T> with column-defined renderers. Fully typed.'
looks_reasonable_because: Column key typed as keyof T
review_summary: Lies about typesafety. any in render and the React.ReactNode cast destroy every keyof T guarantee. key={i} corrupts row state after sort. Comparator returns -1 for equality (unstable). th has no keyboard handling or aria-sort.

issues_found.title: No 0 case — unstable + wrong for strings of numbers
issues_found.explanation: av>bv?1:-1 has no equality case; equal values return -1 = unstable. With keys typed any, '10' < '9' lexicographically — numeric columns sort wrong.
issues_found.root_cause: Naive ternary comparator + any cast hiding string/number ambiguity.
issues_found.fix: Return 0 on equality; branch on typeof. For strings localeCompare; numbers subtract; Dates getTime. Or per-column compare.
issues_found.verification: Sort numbers 2,10,100 — must be numeric order.

issues_found.title: Index key corrupts row identity after sort
issues_found.explanation: After sort, row N is a different logical row; React thinks same id. Per-row uncontrolled state (text inputs, animations) attaches to wrong row. User expands row 3, sorts, now row 3 is a different one.
issues_found.root_cause: Index-as-key when data reorders.
issues_found.fix: Require getRowId: (row: T) => string.
issues_found.verification: Render row with <input> inside, type in row 3, sort — input must follow logical row.

issues_found.title: render uses any
issues_found.explanation: render?: (value: any, row: T) lets render: (v) => v.toFixed(2) compile even if v is a string. keyof T was meant to type value as T[key].
issues_found.root_cause: Reaching for any to defeat generic-narrowing.
issues_found.fix: Mapped/discriminated union: {[K in keyof T]: { key: K; render?: (v: T[K], row: T) => ReactNode; ... }}[keyof T].
issues_found.verification: render for a number column doing .toUpperCase() should TS-error.

issues_found.title: Cast hides non-renderable values
issues_found.explanation: createdAt: Date renders 'Objects are not valid as React child' at runtime. Cast tells TS to shut up; runtime doesn't care.
issues_found.root_cause: Cast as compiler suppression rather than runtime narrowing.
issues_found.fix: Coerce: String(value ?? ''), or require render for non-primitive columns via typed union.
issues_found.verification: Pass Date column with no render — should display string, not throw.

issues_found.title: Sort UI fails keyboard + SR
issues_found.explanation: th isn't focusable; Tab past, Enter does nothing. No aria-sort.
issues_found.root_cause: onClick treated as sufficient interaction.
issues_found.fix: <button> inside th + aria-sort='ascending'|'descending'|'none'.
issues_found.verification: Tab to header, Enter sorts; VoiceOver announces state.

issues_found.title: Widest possible constraint
issues_found.explanation: any in index signature means a React element could be 'T'.
issues_found.root_cause: Loose constraint to dodge errors elsewhere.
issues_found.fix: Record<string, unknown> + per-column typed unions handle narrowing.
issues_found.verification: Non-plain object should error; plain objects still work.

issues_that_arent_issues: w-full is fine. setSortDir updater function — good. Spread before sort — correct.
broader_pattern: any and 'as' aren't type-safety; they're admissions of defeat. In a generic component reaching for them means the generic is wrong. Mapped types and discriminated unions over keyof T keep type info flowing. Every 'as' in a library file is a bug report.
what_makes_this_review_senior: 'Looks generic' isn't 'is generic.' any inside Column<T> passes strict mode but defeats the purpose. Index-key after sort is subtle corruption a senior chases down because they once debugged a P0 where a draft message got sent to the wrong contact via the same root cause.

scenario: Custom dropdown menu (e.g., user account menu in app header).
submitter_context: PR: 'New custom dropdown for account menu. Click-outside closes it. Styled to match design.'
looks_reasonable_because: Looks identical to a real dropdown
review_summary: This is a div pretending to be a menu. Keyboard users can't open it, screen reader users hear nothing meaningful, Esc doesn't close it, focus isn't trapped or restored, and click-outside fires on mousedown of children inside open menus when the click event reaches document. Inaccessible and fragile.

issues_found.title: Trigger is not a button
issues_found.explanation: div with onClick can't be reached by Tab and has no role. Keyboard and SR users cannot open the menu at all. WCAG 2.1.1 violation; many enterprise contracts require this fix before shipping.
issues_found.root_cause: Using divs because they style 'cleaner' than buttons.
issues_found.fix: <button type='button' aria-haspopup='menu' aria-expanded={open} aria-controls=menuId>.
issues_found.verification: Tab through page — focus lands on trigger; Enter/Space opens.

issues_found.title: Items are divs, not menuitems
issues_found.explanation: No role='menuitem', no keyboard support. Up/Down arrows do nothing. SR announces 'group, Profile' instead of 'menu, Profile, menuitem 1 of 3.'
issues_found.root_cause: Same anti-pattern as trigger — divs styled to look like UI.
issues_found.fix: role='menu' on container, role='menuitem' + tabIndex on items. Implement arrow-key navigation and Esc handling.
issues_found.verification: NVDA/VoiceOver announces 'menu, 3 items'; arrows traverse items.

issues_found.title: document.click handler attached even when menu closed
issues_found.explanation: Listener attached for the component's life — wasteful and adds a global listener even when menu never opens. With dozens of menus on the page, the listener overhead compounds. Bigger problem: the handler runs on every document click including bubbled clicks from items, which fires twice (item handler + outside check — outside check sees the item is INSIDE so returns, but the cost is real).
issues_found.root_cause: Coupling lifecycle to the component, not to the open state.
issues_found.fix: Add listener only when open=true. Use 'pointerdown' (fires before click) to close before the item's click reaches React. Or use the closest dialog/popover element which the browser handles natively.
issues_found.verification: Open DevTools event listeners panel; only one listener attached when open.

issues_found.title: No Esc to close, no focus return
issues_found.explanation: Open menu, press Esc — nothing happens. Click an item, menu closes, focus is lost (back to body), so the next Tab starts from the top of the page. For sighted keyboard users this is disorienting; for SR users it's worse.
issues_found.root_cause: Modeling menu as toggle-able UI rather than a focus context.
issues_found.fix: onKeyDown for Esc closing + return focus to the trigger ref. Focus first menu item on open.
issues_found.verification: Open menu (keyboard), Esc closes, focus back on trigger. Same after selecting an item.

issues_found.title: No type='button'... well, it's a div, so it's all wrong
issues_found.explanation: Bundled with #1. Worth flagging that even if changed to <button>, must be type='button' — inside a form, a default-type submit button would submit the form when the user opens the menu.
issues_found.root_cause: Same.
issues_found.fix: Explicit type='button'.
issues_found.verification: Place menu inside a <form> in dev; opening must not submit.

issues_found.title: Menu can clip off screen on narrow viewports
issues_found.explanation: right-0 hard-anchors to right edge; on narrow viewports near the edge of the screen the menu clips. No flip behavior.
issues_found.root_cause: Static positioning where dynamic placement is needed.
issues_found.fix: Use floating-ui (or Radix Popover) for collision-aware placement.
issues_found.verification: Resize viewport so trigger is near edge — menu must stay on screen.

issues_that_arent_issues: Background color blue-500 is style not a11y issue (contrast of the avatar dot vs page doesn't matter for navigation). 'use client' directive omitted is fine for examples.
broader_pattern: Custom interactive UI is keyboard-first and SR-first, or it's broken. Either use a primitives library (Radix, Headless UI, React Aria) — which is the right answer 95% of the time — or implement the full WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices pattern (roles, keyboard nav, focus management, Esc, click-outside, type-ahead). The middle ground is shipping bugs to users with disabilities.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The strongest signal of seniority here is 'just use Radix.' The instinct to hand-roll comes from juniors wanting to learn or designers wanting full control; a senior says no, this is a primitive that took the industry a decade to get right — use the library and customize. Catching pointerdown vs click for outside-detection is the kind of detail that comes from a bug where 'menu closed before item click fired.' Focus return is invisible to most reviewers but obvious to anyone who has navigated by keyboard.

scenario: App-wide ThemeContext that exposes theme + setTheme to the whole tree.
submitter_context: PR: 'Theme context for light/dark. Wraps the app at the root. Components can useTheme().'
review_summary: Two anti-patterns. The provider value is a fresh object each render (no useMemo), so every consumer re-renders on any state change — even consumers reading only theme. Also it's a god-context: theme and user state are unrelated and should be separate contexts. The null-as-any default hides missing-provider errors at runtime.

issues_found.title: New object every render invalidates all consumers
issues_found.explanation: value={{ theme, setTheme, user, setUser }} creates a new reference each render. Context comparison is by reference, so every component using useTheme re-renders on any parent re-render — even when nothing they care about changed. With 500 consumers across the app, every theme toggle re-renders every consumer (acceptable), but every user state change also re-renders every theme consumer (bad).
issues_found.root_cause: Context value identity = referential equality. Inline object literals always change.
issues_found.fix: Wrap value in useMemo with the right deps. Better: split into ThemeContext and UserContext.
issues_found.verification: Add React DevTools 'highlight renders' — toggle user, theme-only consumers should not flash.

issues_found.title: Unrelated state lumped together
issues_found.explanation: Theme and user have different change cadences and consumer sets. Lumping them means every user-only component re-renders on theme change and vice versa. Also makes the file impossible to split as the app grows.
issues_found.root_cause: Convenience win in early scaffolding; never refactored.
issues_found.fix: Split contexts.
issues_found.verification: Re-render audit.

issues_found.title: null as any default hides missing provider
issues_found.explanation: If a consumer mounts outside AppProvider (e.g., a unit test, a Storybook story), useTheme returns null. The first .theme access throws TypeError: Cannot read properties of null. Stack trace is unhelpful; root cause (no provider) is invisible.
issues_found.root_cause: Sloppy default to satisfy TS without runtime safety.
issues_found.fix: useTheme should throw with a clear message: 'useTheme must be used within ThemeProvider.'
issues_found.verification: Render a component without provider — should throw a meaningful error.

issues_found.title: Theme resets on reload
issues_found.explanation: useState initial 'light' means every reload defaults to light, even if the user previously chose dark. UX bug.
issues_found.root_cause: State without persistence.
issues_found.fix: Read from localStorage on init; sync on change. Also respect prefers-color-scheme media query as default.
issues_found.verification: Toggle to dark, reload — must remain dark.

issues_found.title: No SSR/hydration handling
issues_found.explanation: If this runs in Next.js or Remix, the server renders 'light' (no localStorage); client reads dark; hydration mismatch warns or worse, content flashes light then dark (FOUC).
issues_found.root_cause: Reading client-only state during SSR.
issues_found.fix: Apply theme via a sync inline <script> in <head> that sets a class on <html> before React hydrates. React then mirrors that.
issues_found.verification: View source — html element should already carry class='dark' before bundle runs.

issues_found.title: Hardcoded two values
issues_found.explanation: Many apps grow to system / sepia / high-contrast.
issues_found.root_cause: Premature limitation.
issues_found.fix: Type Theme as 'light' | 'dark' | 'system'; resolve to actual on read.
issues_found.verification: Add 'system' value and confirm follows OS preference.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using useContext as a one-liner export is fine. Not using useSyncExternalStore is fine here.
broader_pattern: Context is a dependency-injection mechanism, not a state-management library. Use it for stable values (theme, locale, auth user identity); split by change cadence; memoize the value; and never use it for high-frequency state. When you find yourself memoizing every consumer, you're using Context wrong — switch to a real store (Zustand/Jotai/Redux).
what_makes_this_review_senior: Catching the inline-object-literal in Provider value separates seniors from juniors — it's a real production cost you only feel after profiling a 'why is the app slow' ticket. Splitting contexts by change cadence comes from refactoring teams out of god-context hell. The SSR/FOUC bit is hard-won knowledge from Next.js apps: the only way to avoid the flash is a sync <script> before React, which contradicts everything about 'do it the React way.'

scenario: Signup form: email, password, submit. POSTs to /api/signup.
submitter_context: PR: 'Signup form with client-side validation. Disabled while submitting. Tested happy path.'
review_summary: Looks tidy but ships at least six issues. Validation is performative (e.includes('@') accepts 'a@'), password field isn't type='password' (visible plaintext + browser doesn't offer save), no autoComplete hints, error state isn't accessible to SR, double-submit possible if user presses Enter twice fast, and full-page redirect via window.location instead of router navigation drops SPA state and bypasses any auth context refresh.

issues_found.title: Password input is type='text' — visible
issues_found.explanation: Default type is text. Password appears on screen, in screen capture tools, in browser password autofill flows (which won't recognize it as a password field). Also keylogger-friendly. Browser won't offer to save the password to keychain.
issues_found.root_cause: Forgetting type='password' is shockingly common; default-text bites.
issues_found.fix: type='password'. Also add autoComplete='new-password' for signup (vs current-password for login).
issues_found.verification: Inspect element — must be type='password'; Chrome should offer to save.

issues_found.title: email.includes('@') is not validation
issues_found.explanation: Accepts 'a@', '@', '@@@'. The server will reject these, but the client accepts. UX is bad and the validation is theater.
issues_found.root_cause: Hand-rolled validation. Email validation is famously hard.
issues_found.fix: Use HTML5 type='email' + required and rely on the browser. For complex needs use Zod/Yup with React Hook Form.
issues_found.verification: Try 'a@', 'foo' — must fail; 'foo@bar.com' — must pass.

issues_found.title: Double-submit race
issues_found.explanation: setSubmitting(true) is async. If user hits Enter twice in <1 frame, both handleSubmit calls pass the submitting guard (which reads stale state). Two POSTs fire; if server creates account on first and returns 200, second returns 'email exists' error and the user sees a confusing failure on a successful signup.
issues_found.root_cause: Reading setState-updated value in the same synchronous flow.
issues_found.fix: useTransition or a useRef boolean lock that you check synchronously at the top of handleSubmit.
issues_found.verification: Hammer Enter — Network tab must show exactly one POST.

issues_found.title: Full-page redirect drops SPA state
issues_found.explanation: In a router-driven app (React Router, Next.js, TanStack Router), window.location.href reloads the whole bundle, dropping any in-memory state, scroll position, auth context. Slower navigation and a UX downgrade.
issues_found.root_cause: Habit from non-SPA forms.
issues_found.fix: useNavigate() / router.push('/welcome'). Trigger auth context refresh first so the new route renders authenticated UI without a flicker.
issues_found.verification: DevTools — no full page reload; auth context shows logged-in user immediately.

issues_found.title: Errors not announced to screen readers
issues_found.explanation:
{error}
appears visually but SR users get no announcement. With server validation errors, blind users won't know why submit failed.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating error as cosmetic, not a status update.
issues_found.fix: role='alert' (or aria-live='polite'/'assertive' on a stable region). Also associate inline errors with their inputs via aria-describedby.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver — submit invalid, must hear the error announced.

issues_found.title: No autoComplete hints
issues_found.explanation: Email field should have autoComplete='email'; password autoComplete='new-password'. Without them, password managers and browser autofill behave unpredictably.
issues_found.root_cause: Forgetting form metadata.
issues_found.fix: Add autoComplete to both.
issues_found.verification: 1Password / Chrome autofill recognizes the form.

issues_found.title: setSubmitting(false) not in finally — won't run if fetch throws
issues_found.explanation: Network drop = fetch rejects, setSubmitting(false) never runs, button stuck disabled.
issues_found.root_cause: Async without try/finally.
issues_found.fix: try/catch/finally.
issues_found.verification: Kill server mid-submit — button must re-enable, error must show.

issues_that_arent_issues: No CSRF token in body — for /api/signup which doesn't yet have a session, this is fine; CSRF is for state-changing actions on authenticated endpoints. preventDefault correctly used.
broader_pattern: Forms are not 'input + button + onSubmit.' They're: HTML5 input types and attributes, controlled state, async lock (no double-submit), validation that matches server semantics, accessible error reporting, autofill hints, and SPA-correct navigation. The HTML5 attributes do half the work for free if you let them. Reach for React Hook Form + Zod for any non-trivial form — DIY forms are an endless source of small bugs.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The sync lock vs setState lock for double-submit is the bug that makes a senior write 'this passes manual QA and breaks on slow networks.' window.location.href is one of those tells: it works but signals the author didn't think about SPA state. And the e.includes('@') line is the kind of validation that always passes review unless someone has handled the customer ticket 'I can't log in' three times because 'my email is a@'.

scenario: A modal that slides in from the right with a width transition.
submitter_context: PR: 'Slide-in modal. Smooth 300ms animation. CSS transitions via Tailwind.'
review_summary: Three problems compound. Animating 'width' is a layout-triggering animation — runs on CPU at maybe 20fps. The conditional render means the element mounts already at w-96 (no closed-to-open transition), so the animation never plays. And dismount removes it instantly — no exit animation. Plus a11y issues: not a real modal (no focus trap, no role='dialog', backdrop click missing, Esc missing).

issues_found.title: Animating width is layout-bound, not GPU-accelerated
issues_found.explanation: Width changes recalculate layout for every frame and re-layout descendants. At 60fps that's 16ms per frame to recompute — on a content-heavy panel you'll see 20-30fps on mid-tier devices. Should animate transform: translateX which is GPU-composited and ~free.
issues_found.root_cause: Animating layout properties (width/height/top/left/margin/padding) instead of compositor properties (transform/opacity).
issues_found.fix: Translate from translate-x-full to translate-x-0. Width is fixed; the panel just slides in/out.
issues_found.verification: DevTools Performance — fps stays at 60, no 'Layout' events in transition span.

issues_found.title: Conditional render kills the enter animation
issues_found.explanation: When open flips to true, the element mounts with the open className already applied. No 'from' state to transition from. The animation is invisible. Same in reverse: unmount removes the element instantly.
issues_found.root_cause: Mount/unmount and transition can't both happen; transitions need an existing element to change state.
issues_found.fix: Render always, toggle the open class. Or use the View Transitions API in React 19 (startTransition + viewTransition). Or framer-motion AnimatePresence.
issues_found.verification: Watch the panel open — must visibly slide in, not pop in.

issues_found.title: Not actually a modal — no a11y semantics
issues_found.explanation: No role='dialog', no aria-modal='true', no focus trap, no Esc handling, no backdrop, no focus restore on close. SR announces 'group' or nothing. Keyboard user opens it and tab continues into the rest of the page behind.
issues_found.root_cause: Calling something a 'modal' visually without implementing the modal pattern.
issues_found.fix: <dialog> element (native), or Radix Dialog. If hand-rolled: role='dialog' aria-modal='true' aria-labelledby; focus trap; Esc to close; backdrop click; focus return.
issues_found.verification: WAI-ARIA dialog conformance check.

issues_found.title: transition-all is wasteful
issues_found.explanation: transition-all forces the browser to track every property on the element for changes — including ones added by parent state (e.g., a debug outline). Higher overhead than necessary, and can produce unintended transitions when an unrelated property changes.
issues_found.root_cause: Wildcard transition habit.
issues_found.fix: transition-transform or transition-[transform] in Tailwind v4 arbitrary value syntax.
issues_found.verification: DevTools — transition is exclusively on transform.

issues_found.title: No scroll lock, no backdrop
issues_found.explanation: User scrolls behind the panel; mobile users can't tell the panel is modal. Standard expectation.
issues_found.root_cause: Missing pieces of the modal pattern.
issues_found.fix: Backdrop element with opacity transition; document.body.style.overflow = 'hidden' while open (or scroll-lock libs).
issues_found.verification: Open panel, scroll outside it — body must not scroll.

issues_found.title: Close button lacks accessible name
issues_found.explanation: 'Close' as text is okay but if it becomes an icon button later, aria-label='Close panel' is mandatory. Worth setting the pattern.
issues_found.root_cause: Easy to forget when the visible label exists.
issues_found.fix: Even now, aria-label can supplement for clarity ('Close panel').
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces 'Close panel, button.'

issues_that_arent_issues: fixed positioning is correct. shadow-lg is fine. Tailwind class concatenation via template literal is fine.
broader_pattern: Animate only transform and opacity. Anything else costs layout/paint and degrades on lower-end devices. For mount/unmount with animations, render the element always and toggle state, or use a presence library. And 'modal' is a contract, not a div with high z-index — implement the full pattern or use a library that does.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The width-vs-transform distinction is foundational but ignored constantly. Catching that conditional render breaks the enter transition is a junior-eye-roll bug that only shows up when you actually open the dev tools and watch the animation play. And reaching for native <dialog> (newly viable since 2024 baseline) instead of yet another hand-rolled modal is the senior move — it gets focus trap, Esc, backdrop click for free.

scenario: A date-picker component that formats dates and shows a calendar.
submitter_context: PR: 'New DatePicker using moment and lodash for date formatting and helpers.'
review_summary: ~600KB+ of bundle for what should be a small component. moment is 290KB and not tree-shakable. import _ from 'lodash' imports the whole library. import * as Icons from 'lucide-react' defeats tree-shaking of icons. And moment is in maintenance mode — the project recommends migrating away.

issues_found.title: moment.js is ~290KB minified and is deprecated
issues_found.explanation: The moment team officially recommends moving to date-fns, Luxon, or Day.js. moment is also stateful (timezone, locale globals) and not tree-shakable. Including it alone may bust your performance budget.
issues_found.root_cause: Reaching for the well-known library by reflex.
issues_found.fix: date-fns (tree-shakable, ~2KB per used function) or Day.js (~7KB total). Both have moment-compatible APIs.
issues_found.verification: webpack-bundle-analyzer — moment should be 0 bytes after change.

issues_found.title: Imports the entire lodash package
issues_found.explanation: _.range alone is 10 lines of plain JS. Importing the default brings every lodash function whether used or not. 75KB+ in the bundle.
issues_found.root_cause: Default import habit. lodash supports per-function imports but most devs don't know.
issues_found.fix: import range from 'lodash/range' for tree-shaking, or write _.range(0, 31) as Array.from({ length: 31 }, (_, i) => i). Native is sufficient for trivial use.
issues_found.verification: Bundle analyzer — lodash drops to a few KB or zero.

issues_found.title: Namespace import defeats tree-shaking
issues_found.explanation: import * forces the bundler to include every icon. lucide-react has hundreds — ~200KB. Even with side-effect free declarations, namespace imports prevent dead-code elimination.
issues_found.root_cause: Convenience of Icons.Foo namespacing.
issues_found.fix: import { Calendar } from 'lucide-react'.
issues_found.verification: Bundle — only the icons you use are present.

issues_found.title: Always renders 31 days regardless of month length
issues_found.explanation: _.range(0, 31) plus startOf('month').add(d, 'days') overflows into the next month for February. The calendar UI shows '2025-03-03' inside February's grid.
issues_found.root_cause: Hardcoding month length.
issues_found.fix: Compute days using endOf('month').date() (or date-fns getDaysInMonth).
issues_found.verification: Open in February — must show 28 (or 29) cells, not 31.

issues_found.title: Locale-insensitive format leaks technical date format to users
issues_found.explanation: YYYY-MM-DD is fine for backends; users in many locales expect DD/MM/YYYY or MMM D, YYYY. Hardcoding strips locale, screen reader announces awkwardly.
issues_found.root_cause: Using ISO format for UI text.
issues_found.fix: Intl.DateTimeFormat or date-fns format with locale.
issues_found.verification: Switch browser locale to fr-FR; date should display in French format.

issues_found.title: Inside a form, this opens the calendar AND submits
issues_found.explanation: DatePickers commonly live in forms. Without type='button', clicking 'Open' submits the form.
issues_found.root_cause: Default button type is 'submit' inside form.
issues_found.fix: type='button' on every button that isn't the submit.
issues_found.verification: Put inside a <form onSubmit={...}>, click trigger — must not submit.

issues_that_arent_issues: className='relative' is fine. grid-cols-7 is the canonical week grid. Closing on selection is reasonable UX.
broader_pattern: Every dependency is a bundle, runtime, security, and maintenance cost. Audit imports by (a) is it tree-shakable, (b) is the library in maintenance mode, (c) is this trivially replaceable with native APIs, (d) how big is it. moment, lodash, and namespace imports of icon libs are the three most common bundle bloat sources in React apps in 2026.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Spotting moment.js without checking bundle is dated reflex; spotting it AND knowing the project is on maintenance is current knowledge. The 31-days-overflow bug is the kind of thing only senior reviewers catch because they've shipped a date picker before and remember the embarrassment of an internal stakeholder finding 'Feb 30' in QA.

scenario: App-wide ErrorBoundary wrapping all routes.
submitter_context: PR: 'Catch-all ErrorBoundary. Shows friendly error page instead of white screen.'
looks_reasonable_because: Standard ErrorBoundary class pattern from React docs
review_summary: This boundary doesn't catch what real apps throw. Async errors, event handler errors, promises, errors during SSR — none caught. No recovery (user is stuck on 'Something went wrong' until they manually reload). No error reporting to Sentry/etc. componentDidCatch swallows the errorInfo. And it catches but doesn't reset on route change.

issues_found.title: Doesn't catch async errors, event handlers, or promise rejections
issues_found.explanation: React error boundaries only catch render-phase, lifecycle, and constructor errors. fetch().then(...).catch(undefined) errors don't reach the boundary. onClick handler throwing — not caught. async function inside useEffect throwing — not caught (unless you await and re-throw in render). Most production errors are async; this boundary misses them.
issues_found.root_cause: Misunderstanding the boundary contract — it catches the React tree's render path, not all JS errors.
issues_found.fix: Add window.addEventListener('error') and 'unhandledrejection' listeners in a parent component to bridge async errors to a state flag that triggers fallback. Or use react-error-boundary library and accompanying use-error-handler hook to surface async errors to render.
issues_found.verification: Throw inside a setTimeout — boundary should still show fallback.

issues_found.title: No telemetry / Sentry integration
issues_found.explanation: console.error is invisible in production. Without sending to Sentry/Datadog/Bugsnag, you have no idea your app is crashing for users. Errors slip silently.
issues_found.root_cause: Logging local only.
issues_found.fix: Call Sentry.captureException(error, { extra: errorInfo }). Include user id, route, app version.
issues_found.verification: Throw in dev with Sentry test DSN — error appears in Sentry within seconds.

issues_found.title: No recovery — user is stuck
issues_found.explanation: Once hasError=true, the only way out is full reload. If error was transient (network blip), the user shouldn't have to refresh.
issues_found.root_cause: Boundary as terminal state rather than retry point.
issues_found.fix: Provide reset() that sets hasError back to false; render a 'Try again' button. Also use a key prop on the boundary that changes on route change so it resets automatically.
issues_found.verification: Trigger error, click reset, app returns to a working state.

issues_found.title: Boundary stuck across navigation
issues_found.explanation: User errors on /profile, navigates to /home — error boundary still showing because state isn't reset. Many apps wrap the entire app, so navigation feels broken.
issues_found.root_cause: Boundary state is bound to component instance not route.
issues_found.fix: Pass a key={pathname} to the boundary; pathname change unmounts/remounts and resets.
issues_found.verification: Trigger error, navigate to another route — boundary resets.

issues_found.title: componentDidCatch ignores errorInfo (component stack)
issues_found.explanation: componentDidCatch(error, errorInfo) — the errorInfo.componentStack is the React tree path where the error occurred. Critical for debugging. Discarding it means Sentry stack traces are vague.
issues_found.root_cause: Not using the second argument.
issues_found.fix: componentDidCatch(error, errorInfo) and include errorInfo.componentStack in telemetry.
issues_found.verification: Trigger render error in nested component — Sentry should show React component stack.

issues_found.title: Fallback is not accessible
issues_found.explanation: Plain <div> with no role. SR users get no announcement that something went wrong.
issues_found.root_cause: Minimal fallback.
issues_found.fix: role='alert' on the fallback container. Provide context: 'An unexpected error occurred. Try again or reload.'
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver — should announce error.

issues_found.title: Doesn't work for SSR errors
issues_found.explanation: During SSR (Next.js), ErrorBoundary catches client-side render errors but the SSR pipeline has different mechanisms (error.tsx in App Router). If app uses SSR, this boundary alone is insufficient.
issues_found.root_cause: Single boundary for a multi-context app.
issues_found.fix: Layer: app/error.tsx for SSR + this boundary for CSR. Document the split.
issues_found.verification: Server-render a page that throws — Next's error.tsx must render.

issues_that_arent_issues: Class component is the only way — there's no functional API for boundaries yet. console.error in dev is fine alongside Sentry.
broader_pattern: Error boundaries are a piece of the error story, not the story. Cover render errors (boundary), async errors (window listeners + react-error-boundary), SSR errors (framework-specific), and never forget telemetry. A boundary without recovery and reporting is theater.
what_makes_this_review_senior: 'It looks like the docs example' is the senior tell that the boundary won't survive real production. Knowing that async errors don't reach the boundary is foundational; knowing to bridge them via window.error + unhandledrejection is one step deeper. The reset-on-route-change is a customer-support driven insight: users get stuck on the error screen because no one thought about navigation as a recovery mechanism.

scenario: Hook that polls an endpoint at a configurable interval, exposing the latest data.
submitter_context: PR: 'usePolling hook for live data. Polls every interval. Cleans up.'
looks_reasonable_because: Uses async function inside effect — common pattern
review_summary: The dependency array lies. transform is read inside the effect but excluded from deps. If the consumer passes a new transform function (which they will, every render, because it's likely inline), the effect won't re-run but tick() captures the original transform forever. Plus: setInterval timing drift, no error handling, no document.hidden pause, and any-typed json defeats the generic.

issues_found.title: transform missing from dep array (stale closure)
issues_found.explanation: Every render, the consumer passes a fresh transform function (since arrow functions inline). The effect captures the first transform and uses it forever. When consumer's transform depends on other state (closes over user.locale, say), polling delivers data formatted for the original locale. Silent bug.
issues_found.root_cause: Excluding non-primitive deps from arrays because 'they change every render.' The fix is making them stable, not omitting them.
issues_found.fix: Either accept the re-run (and document that transform should be useCallback'd), or store transform in a ref that the effect reads. Best: useEffectEvent (React 19+) so the effect doesn't re-subscribe but always reads the latest transform.
issues_found.verification: Pass a transform that captures changing state — polled data must reflect the latest transform.

issues_found.title: setInterval drifts and can stack
issues_found.explanation: setInterval fires regardless of whether the previous tick finished. If the request takes longer than interval, multiple tick() calls overlap. With a backend slowdown, you can have 10 in-flight requests at once. Also, when tab is backgrounded, browsers throttle setInterval irregularly, so 'every 5s' becomes 'every minute or so.'
issues_found.root_cause: setInterval semantics don't wait for the work.
issues_found.fix: setTimeout chained after the tick completes (or fails). Cleaner: schedule next tick in finally().
issues_found.verification: Add 10s artificial delay to fetch — Network panel should never show overlapping requests.

issues_found.title: Failed tick rejects unhandled
issues_found.explanation: fetch rejects (network drop) → tick rejects → unhandledrejection. Polling stops if using chained setTimeout (no next schedule); silent if using setInterval (which keeps firing but you set up no error path).
issues_found.root_cause: Async work without try/catch.
issues_found.fix: try/catch in tick. Surface error state. Implement exponential backoff on consecutive failures.
issues_found.verification: Disable network — polling should pause/backoff, then resume on reconnect.

issues_found.title: Wastes battery and bandwidth in background tabs
issues_found.explanation: User leaves tab open for hours, polling continues at full rate. Mobile drains battery; on metered networks costs money. Server gets unnecessary load.
issues_found.root_cause: No visibility integration.
issues_found.fix: document.visibilityState === 'hidden' → pause; 'visible' → resume + immediate tick.
issues_found.verification: DevTools background tab simulation — polling stops while hidden.

issues_found.title: any in transform signature
issues_found.explanation: transform?: (data: any) => T accepts anything; runtime type errors slip through.
issues_found.root_cause: any in public API.
issues_found.fix: Make transform required when generic differs, or take unknown and let consumer narrow. Better: take a Zod schema.
issues_found.verification: Pass a transform expecting wrong shape — should TS-fail or runtime-validate.

issues_found.title: data not reset on url change
issues_found.explanation: When url changes, the effect re-runs but data state still holds the old url's data until first new tick completes. Brief flash of wrong data.
issues_found.root_cause: State decoupled from current url.
issues_found.fix: setData(null) in the effect's start, or track loading state.
issues_found.verification: Toggle url — data must clear before first new tick.

issues_that_arent_issues: active flag is correct and good. Calling tick() immediately on mount is right (don't wait one interval). Generic T usage is fine.
broader_pattern: Dependency arrays are not 'list of things that changed.' They're the contract: 'rerun effect when any of these change.' Excluding a dep that the effect actually reads is a stale-closure bug — every time. For non-stable deps (callbacks, objects), make them stable upstream, store in a ref, or use useEffectEvent. setInterval is almost never what you want for polling; chained setTimeout is.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Stale-closure-via-missing-dep is the most common React bug in production and the most-debated lint rule. A senior knows the fix isn't to lie to the linter (// eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps) but to stabilize the dep or move it to a ref. setInterval-vs-chained-setTimeout for polling is hard-won knowledge — most devs write setInterval until they debug overlapping requests in prod.

scenario: OrderDetail page: header, line items, totals, address, payment, history. All in one component.
submitter_context: PR: 'Order detail page. Renders everything for an order.'
looks_reasonable_because: Works in dev with fake data
review_summary: God component: 9 useStates, 3 effects coupled in one fetch, two modals inline, business logic mixed with rendering. Every concern is reachable from every other, so a bug in refunds re-renders the address editor and the history list. Plus 'any' everywhere, missing optimistic updates, no error handling, totals recomputed on every render, and a wrong endpoint URL (orderId used as userId).

issues_found.title: Bug: orderId used as userId in URL
issues_found.explanation: fetch(`/api/users/${orderId}/payment-methods`) — orderId is not a userId. Currently returns empty or 404, masked because the response goes through .then(r => r.json()) without an ok check. UI shows 'no payment methods' regardless of the customer's actual cards.
issues_found.root_cause: Copy-paste with name collision. No type safety on URL params.
issues_found.fix: Use order.customer.id (loaded from order fetch). Sequence: load order first, then payments. Or join on the backend.
issues_found.verification: Order with known payment methods — UI must show them.

issues_found.title: order/history/paymentMethods typed as any
issues_found.explanation: order.customer.name compiles even if customer is undefined. order.items.map crashes if items is null on an unusual order (e.g., subscription orders). No autocomplete; refactor-hostile.
issues_found.root_cause: Untyped fetch responses.
issues_found.fix: Type the API responses (generated from OpenAPI, or hand-typed). Validate at the boundary with Zod.
issues_found.verification: Make API return a subscription order with no items — UI handles gracefully.

issues_found.title: Address, refund, payments, history all in one file
issues_found.explanation: Every state interaction couples to every render. Edit address re-renders refund modal logic. History list re-fetches when refund modal opens (due to shared re-render path). File is 200 lines and will be 800 in a year. Impossible to test or reuse pieces.
issues_found.root_cause: Building outward instead of decomposing.
issues_found.fix: Extract <OrderHeader />, <OrderItems />, <OrderAddressEditor />, <RefundDialog />, <OrderHistory />. Each takes the data it needs. Probably each fetches its own data via React Query so they're independent.
issues_found.verification: Render OrderHistory in Storybook — should work standalone.

issues_found.title: Sequential fire-and-forget fetches with no error handling
issues_found.explanation: Three fetches kicked off in parallel, no awaiting, no error handling, no abort on unmount. Any one failing leaves the matching state at initial value. .then(r => r.json()) ignores response.ok. Component shows partial UI silently.
issues_found.root_cause: Naive promise chaining without error propagation.
issues_found.fix: Use TanStack Query with three queries. Errors, retries, cache, dedupe — all free. Per-section loading states.
issues_found.verification: 500 the history endpoint — that section shows error, rest still works.

issues_found.title: No optimistic UI, no error handling, no validation
issues_found.explanation: Click Save → modal closes immediately on resolution but if API fails, user thinks address saved when it didn't. issueRefund accepts any string as amount; '99.99 USD' or 'abc' will POST. Server validation rejects but user gets no feedback. No idempotency key — clicking refund twice issues two refunds.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating mutations as fire-and-forget.
issues_found.fix: Mutation library (TanStack), with onError to roll back, idempotency key in headers, client-side numeric validation, success toasts.
issues_found.verification: Server throws on save — UI must restore prior address and surface error.

issues_found.title: Total recomputed inline
issues_found.explanation: order.items.reduce(...) runs on every render. Cheap for small carts, real for 200-line orders.
issues_found.root_cause: Computation in render with no memoization.
issues_found.fix: useMemo, or compute server-side (preferred — server is source of truth on money).
issues_found.verification: Profile render — total computation absent from re-renders that don't change items.

issues_found.title: Modal lacks dialog semantics
issues_found.explanation: Plain div, no focus management, no Esc. Same as review-005.
issues_found.root_cause: Same.
issues_found.fix: Use a Dialog primitive.
issues_found.verification: Same.

issues_that_arent_issues: Sequential extraction into smaller components is the right call but not strictly a 'bug' — flag it as architecture rather than blocker. CSS not addressed because it's not the review focus.
broader_pattern: Components that fetch >1 unrelated thing or that render >1 distinct feature should be split. The litmus test: if you can describe two responsibilities with the word 'and,' it's two components. Co-location of state and rendering is good; co-location of unrelated state is technical debt. And for any money-touching mutation, you need: validation, idempotency, optimistic with rollback, and error surface — none are optional.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The orderId-as-userId bug is the single most useful catch — it shows seniority in reading what code actually does vs what it looks like it does. The 'split this into 6 components' note is judgment: juniors hear 'too long' and don't know where to cut; seniors cut by data dependency. The money-validation + idempotency notes are the kind of thing only seniors who have caused (or prevented) a duplicate-refund incident think to call out.

scenario: Markdown comments rendered in a public forum.
submitter_context: PR: 'Render comment markdown to HTML. Uses marked. Supports basic formatting.'
looks_reasonable_because: Tests likely pass with benign markdown
review_summary: XSS waiting to happen. marked doesn't sanitize by default; raw <script>, <iframe>, onerror handlers in user markdown execute in the user's session. profileUrl is also unvalidated — javascript: URLs and unrestricted target='_blank' let comments steal sessions or open malicious sites with window.opener access. This component is the textbook frontend security failure.

issues_found.title: XSS via raw HTML in markdown
issues_found.explanation: Markdown specs allow raw HTML pass-through. A comment containing renders as an image that fires the handler. With session cookies httpOnly, the attacker grabs CSRF tokens, exfiltrates DOM secrets, or hijacks the page. Most damaging in a forum where attacker controls comment input.
issues_found.root_cause: Trusting user input as HTML.
issues_found.fix: Sanitize with DOMPurify after marked. Or use react-markdown which doesn't allow raw HTML by default. Or set marked options to disable HTML (sanitize: true was removed in v4+, so use a wrapper).
issues_found.verification: Post a comment with — must not alert, must show literal text or stripped tag.

issues_found.title: javascript: URLs in user-controlled href
issues_found.explanation: If profileUrl comes from user-editable profile (e.g., 'website' field), an attacker sets profileUrl='javascript:fetch(...)'. Clicking the author name executes attacker code.
issues_found.root_cause: Trusting URL strings from user data.
issues_found.fix: Validate scheme: only http/https/mailto. Reject javascript:, data:, vbscript:.
issues_found.verification: Set profileUrl to javascript:alert(1) and click — must do nothing / no JS execution.

issues_found.title: Missing rel='noopener noreferrer' on external links
issues_found.explanation: If profileUrl points off-site and you ever add target='_blank' (or marked autolinks do), the new page has window.opener access — can redirect the original tab to a phishing page (tabnabbing). Also referrer leaks.
issues_found.root_cause: Forgetting the noopener pattern; relying on browser defaults (which only protect target='_blank' since 2020+).
issues_found.fix: rel='noopener noreferrer' on any external anchor, especially target='_blank'.
issues_found.verification: Open child tab via window.opener — must be null.

issues_found.title: Links inside markdown also need sanitization
issues_found.explanation: Markdown [click me](javascript:alert(1)) compiles to <a href='javascript:...'> in marked. Same XSS as #2 but in user content. Even with marked's link sanitizer, edge cases exist (encoded schemes).
issues_found.root_cause: Trusting library to sanitize when it doesn't fully.
issues_found.fix: DOMPurify post-process with allowed protocols list.
issues_found.verification: Markdown link to javascript: — rendered href must be neutralized.

issues_found.title: DOMPurify needs a DOM at SSR
issues_found.explanation: If using Next.js SSR, DOMPurify needs a window. Naive use throws server-side. Devs reach for skipping sanitization on the server 'because attackers can't run JS server-side' — but the HTML reaches the client unsanitized.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating sanitization as a client-only concern.
issues_found.fix: Use isomorphic-dompurify or jsdom. Sanitize on both sides.
issues_found.verification: View-source on a malicious comment — must not contain script/onerror.

issues_found.title: No CSP headers documented
issues_found.explanation: This is a server config concern but worth flagging in review. A strict CSP would mitigate XSS even if sanitization missed something.
issues_found.root_cause: Sanitization without defense-in-depth.
issues_found.fix: Coordinate with backend to add Content-Security-Policy with restrictive script-src.
issues_found.verification: Browser console — strict CSP active.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using <article> for a comment is correct semantic HTML. className for border styling is fine.
broader_pattern: User-generated content is hostile by default. Every place HTML is rendered from user input needs: sanitization (DOMPurify), URL validation (allowlist of protocols), and isolation (noopener on external links). Add CSP as backup. The rule: never pass user data to dangerouslySetInnerHTML without going through a sanitizer.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Knowing marked doesn't sanitize is a frequent gotcha — its README has warnings but devs skim. The javascript: URL angle on profileUrl is a step deeper: most reviewers see the obvious XSS in markdown but miss the same vulnerability in adjacent props. SSR sanitization is the kind of thing a senior remembers from a bug where 'view-source still showed the script tag' even though client-side sanitization worked.

scenario: A 'last seen' timestamp shown on a server-rendered page.
submitter_context: PR: 'Show last-seen as relative time. Uses new Date() and computes diff. Updates every minute.'
review_summary: In SSR, this hydrates with a mismatch. useState(Date.now()) runs on server with server time, then on client with client time — instantly different. React warns of mismatch (or auto-recovers). Also d.toLocaleString() uses the server's locale on SSR and client's locale on hydration. And the 60s interval can show stale times for up to 59s.

issues_found.title: Server and client compute different 'now'
issues_found.explanation: During SSR, useState(Date.now()) runs on the Node server and embeds that ms in the HTML. Client hydrates with a fresh Date.now() — different by at least the round-trip time (often hundreds of ms, more on slow networks). React sees mismatch, warns, and may discard the client tree or re-render. Subtle visible flicker. Worse in React 19's stricter hydration.
issues_found.root_cause: Computing 'now' during render. Render must be deterministic between SSR and CSR hydration.
issues_found.fix: Initialize now from timestamp (or 0) on first render; update in useEffect after mount. Or wrap output in suspense + 'use client' boundary that doesn't SSR this piece.
issues_found.verification: Inspect server-rendered HTML — diff must match client first paint.

issues_found.title: Locale differs between server and client
issues_found.explanation: Server might run en-US, client might be fr-FR. Hydration mismatch. Even on same locale, time zones differ (server UTC vs client local).
issues_found.root_cause: Locale-dependent rendering during SSR.
issues_found.fix: Render absolute fallback as a stable ISO string on server, swap to localized after mount. Or use Intl.DateTimeFormat with explicit locale derived from request headers (not user's actual browser).
issues_found.verification: Render on server with different LANG, hydrate on client — no mismatch warning.

issues_found.title: Stale for up to 59 seconds, no document.hidden pause
issues_found.explanation: Timestamp '5s ago' displayed for up to 59s before next interval. For 'just now' UX that matters. Also runs in background tabs uselessly.
issues_found.root_cause: Coarse interval.
issues_found.fix: Schedule next update at the next 'boundary' (when diff crosses 60s, etc.) using setTimeout with computed delay. Pause on visibility hidden.
issues_found.verification: Watch 'just now' update at exactly 60s, not on a fixed minute cadence.

issues_found.title: Drift accumulates if user keeps the page open for hours
issues_found.explanation: Math.floor((Date.now() - timestamp) / 1000) drifts as setInterval jitters. Reasonable for minutes; visible for hours.
issues_found.root_cause: Fixed-interval polling for what's a derived value.
issues_found.fix: Use Intl.RelativeTimeFormat with the absolute timestamp and compute fresh on every tick (covered by fix above).
issues_found.verification: Leave tab open 1 hour — displayed time matches actual diff.

issues_found.title: No exact timestamp on hover
issues_found.explanation: Users often want exact date. Standard pattern is a relative label with title='exact time' tooltip.
issues_found.root_cause: Missing affordance.
issues_found.fix: <time dateTime={new Date(timestamp).toISOString()} title={new Date(timestamp).toLocaleString()}>...
issues_found.verification: Hover — exact time appears.

issues_found.title: Not using semantic <time> element
issues_found.explanation: <time> is the semantic element for timestamps; SR and date parsers recognize it.
issues_found.root_cause: Habit.
issues_found.fix: Use <time dateTime=ISO>...</time>
issues_found.verification: Validator/HTML inspector recognizes time.

issues_that_arent_issues: useEffect cleanup with clearInterval is correct. Math.floor for human-readable diff is standard.
broader_pattern: During SSR, every render must be deterministic from props and request — no Date.now(), Math.random(), window, localStorage, locale, user agent. If you need dynamic data, render a stable placeholder on server and swap after hydration. The mental rule: 'if I render this twice, does it produce the same HTML?' If no, gate it behind useEffect.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Hydration mismatches on time/locale are the #1 source of 'inexplicable hydration error' tickets in SSR apps. Knowing the rule 'render must be deterministic' and applying it (initialize null, fill in useEffect) is the senior fix. Catching the next-boundary scheduling vs fixed-interval is UX polish you only think to write after the design team has shipped a 'just now' label.

scenario: A FeatureFlags hook that reads from window.__FLAGS__ injected by the server.
submitter_context: PR: 'useFeatureFlag hook. Reads from server-injected globals. Falls back to false.'
review_summary: Works perfectly in dev (where __FLAGS__ is always set early). In production it breaks in three ways: (1) SSR has no window, (2) the script that sets __FLAGS__ may load after the bundle, (3) production minification can rename __FLAGS__ if a Terser config is wrong, leaving a different key on window. And there's no live update if a flag toggle service pushes changes.

issues_found.title: window is undefined during SSR
issues_found.explanation: In Next.js/Remix SSR, the initial useState runs server-side where window doesn't exist — ReferenceError. Even worse: useState lazy initializer makes it appear safe, but the function still runs in the render path. Crash.
issues_found.root_cause: Assuming browser globals exist during render.
issues_found.fix: Guard: typeof window === 'undefined' ? false : window.__FLAGS__?.[key] ?? false. Or use useSyncExternalStore with a server snapshot.
issues_found.verification: SSR a page using this — must not throw.

issues_found.title: Race: __FLAGS__ may be injected after the bundle parses
issues_found.explanation: If the flag script is loaded async (or after the main bundle), the first render reads undefined. The useEffect picks up later, but the first paint shows the wrong UI. Worse: if a critical experience (e.g., new-checkout-flow) depends on the flag, users see the old UI for one frame then a flash to new.
issues_found.root_cause: Synchronous read of an async-loaded global.
issues_found.fix: Block: ensure the flag script is in <head> as a sync script before the bundle. Or wait for a ready event before rendering flag-dependent UI. Or use a hook that subscribes to a promise and returns a loading state.
issues_found.verification: Move flag script to load after bundle — UI should either show loading or correctly resolve flag (not flash).

issues_found.title: Minifier may rename the global
issues_found.explanation: Terser/esbuild with aggressive mangle settings will rename __FLAGS__ if it appears only in one bundle. The server still injects window.__FLAGS__ but the client looks for window.a or similar. Works in dev (no mangle), breaks in prod build only.
issues_found.root_cause: Implicit assumption that property names survive minification.
issues_found.fix: Add a reserved-names config to your bundler ('__FLAGS__' in reserved), OR access via window['__FLAGS__'] which mangles less reliably get touched, OR use a typed global helper that's exported.
issues_found.verification: Build with prod settings, grep bundle for __FLAGS__ — string must appear.

issues_found.title: No subscription — flags can't update at runtime
issues_found.explanation: If a flag service pushes a flag change (LaunchDarkly, etc.) via WebSocket, this hook never sees it because it only re-reads when the key prop changes. The user has to refresh.
issues_found.root_cause: Polling-by-render rather than subscribing.
issues_found.fix: useSyncExternalStore with a subscribe function that listens to a flag-changed event. Update window.__FLAGS__ AND emit an event when the service pushes.
issues_found.verification: Toggle a flag via service — UI updates without reload.

issues_found.title: Hydration mismatch potential
issues_found.explanation: If SSR renders with flag=false (server flag value) but client window has true, the markup differs. Same class of bug as review-014.
issues_found.root_cause: State derived from client-only source during hydration.
issues_found.fix: Have SSR render the same flag value as client (inject the user's flags into HTML). Or render placeholder during SSR.
issues_found.verification: View source — flag-conditional UI matches first client render.

issues_found.title: Returns bare boolean — no loading state
issues_found.explanation: Caller can't distinguish 'flag off' from 'flag not yet loaded.'
issues_found.root_cause: Underspecified return type.
issues_found.fix: Return { value: boolean; ready: boolean } or { value: boolean | undefined } so caller handles loading.
issues_found.verification: Test consumer rendering: ready=false → show skeleton; ready=true → use value.

issues_that_arent_issues: declare global is the right way to type window extensions. Boolean fallback default is conservative-correct.
broader_pattern: 'Works in dev' is a smell. Diff dev vs prod for: SSR (window/document undefined), minification (renamed symbols, dead-code elimination), build-time env vars (process.env.NODE_ENV), error boundaries (different behavior), and source maps. Real test environments must run a production build at least nightly. The rule: if it touches globals, it can break in production only.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Catching minifier-renames-globals is one of those bugs that only senior reviewers think to flag because they've burned a day on a production-only flag bug and the root cause was Terser's mangle. SSR + hydration mismatch on globals is taught everywhere; the script-load-order race is not — most devs assume <script> tags are synchronous-by-default and don't think through what happens when CDN delivery reorders. useSyncExternalStore as the answer for any externally-mutable source is the senior-level pattern in 2026.

scenario: A chat input that maintains drafts per conversation in a parent.
submitter_context: PR: 'ChatInput component. Drafts stored per conversation in parent state.'
review_summary: Every keystroke in ChatInput re-renders the entire conversation list (because setDrafts updates parent state) and every nav button gets re-evaluated. Plus the {...drafts, [activeId]: v} pattern creates a new object on every keystroke that propagates downstream; if any sidebar component memoizes on drafts, it busts. And onSend race-with-onChange: if the user types and presses Enter in the same tick, the empty-out can clobber the latest character.

issues_found.title: Every keystroke re-renders the conversation list
issues_found.explanation: setDrafts triggers ChatApp re-render. Without React.memo on the nav buttons, all conversations re-render. With 200 conversations, that's 200 button re-renders per keystroke — visible jank when typing fast.
issues_found.root_cause: State lifted too high. Drafts only matter to the input; lifting them to the app level forces the whole tree to re-render on each keystroke.
issues_found.fix: Move draft state into ChatInput keyed by activeId, or use a Map ref / store. Or split: useReducer with React.memo on nav.
issues_found.verification: Profiler shows nav buttons not re-rendering on keystroke.

issues_found.title: Stale-state race in onSend then setDrafts after onChange
issues_found.explanation: User types 'h' (setDrafts called with {a:'h'}, async), then immediately Enter. onSend reads drafts (still {} from this closure) and sends ''. Then setDrafts in onSend updates with old + clear. Order depends on closure capture. With fast typing, last char gets dropped.
issues_found.root_cause: Multiple setDrafts calls using non-updater form within a tick read stale state.
issues_found.fix: Use functional updater: setDrafts(d => ({...d, [activeId]: v})). Always — even when 'it works' today.
issues_found.verification: Type fast and Enter immediately — message must contain all typed chars.

issues_found.title: Spreading a large object every keystroke
issues_found.explanation: With many conversations the drafts object can have hundreds of keys. Spread is O(n). On every keystroke. Add allocator pressure to render cycles.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating immutability cheap when key count is large.
issues_found.fix: Use a Map and persist via setState((prev) => { const m = new Map(prev); m.set(activeId, v); return m; }); or scope draft state to per-conversation.
issues_found.verification: Profile keystroke alloc with 1000 conversations — should be flat.

issues_found.title: No keyboard navigation (arrow keys) between conversations
issues_found.explanation: Tab moves through every button — fine. But arrow keys, expected for lists, do nothing. Conversation switching is one of the most-used UI actions; users expect ⌘+arrow or ↑↓.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating list as a series of buttons rather than a roving-tabindex list.
issues_found.fix: role='listbox' or 'tablist' with roving tabindex. Implement ↑↓ keys, Home/End.
issues_found.verification: Keyboard user: arrows traverse conversations.

issues_found.title: conversations[0]?.id can change identity when empty array becomes non-empty
issues_found.explanation: On mount with empty conversations, activeId is undefined. When conversations arrive (async), activeId stays undefined. UI shows no active conversation forever.
issues_found.root_cause: useState only computes initial once.
issues_found.fix: useEffect to set activeId when conversations populate.
issues_found.verification: Start with empty list, load conversations — first one should activate.

issues_found.title: console.log placeholder for production code review
issues_found.explanation: Probably a placeholder, but worth confirming.
issues_found.root_cause: Incomplete PR.
issues_found.fix: Replace with the real send call.
issues_found.verification: PR description should describe what 'send' does.

issues_that_arent_issues: Lifting state to parent is the right call architecturally — fix is to scope, not delete. flex h-screen is standard.
broader_pattern: State should live as low as possible. Lifting state up has a cost: every consumer of that state re-renders. For high-frequency state (input values, cursor positions, scroll), keep it local or use a store with selector-based subscriptions. The 'lift state up' guidance from old docs leads to god-state in big apps.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Diagnosing 'why does typing lag' as a too-high-state problem and not a memoization problem is the senior insight. Juniors reach for React.memo; seniors move the state down. The Map vs object spread perf note is the kind of thing you only see profiling a real chat app under load. The keystroke-then-Enter race is a classic React beginner trap that ships constantly.

scenario: useResizeObserver hook tracking element size.
submitter_context: PR: 'useResizeObserver returns size of a ref'd element. Cleans up.'
review_summary: Three subtle issues. The effect runs once with [] deps — so if the ref's element changes (component re-renders with a different element instance), the observer keeps watching the stale element. The setState on every resize fires inside the observer callback synchronously — can trigger ResizeObserver loop limit warning in some browsers. And setSize creates a new object every callback even when dimensions are identical (after sub-pixel rounding), causing unnecessary re-renders.

issues_found.title: Stale ref capture — observer watches the wrong element after re-mount
issues_found.explanation: useEffect runs once. If the consumer's element is conditionally rendered (e.g., behind a state flag), the first time the effect runs, ref.current may be null. On subsequent renders the element appears but the effect doesn't re-run, so the observer is never set up. Conversely, if the element re-mounts (key change), the observer still watches the old (now detached) element, leaking memory.
issues_found.root_cause: useRef changes are invisible to React; effects can't react to them with deps.
issues_found.fix: Use callback ref (ref as a function) that sets up the observer when the element attaches and tears down on detach. Or use a state-backed ref via useState<T | null>(null).
issues_found.verification: Conditionally render the element. Resize — size must update.

issues_found.title: ResizeObserver loop limit exceeded warnings
issues_found.explanation: If setSize triggers a re-render that changes the observed element's size (common: a panel whose size feeds back into its own layout), ResizeObserver fires again, loops, and the browser logs 'ResizeObserver loop limit exceeded' (Chrome) or silently throttles. Sentry/etc. catches it as an uncaught error in some configs and floods the dashboard.
issues_found.root_cause: Synchronous setState in observer callback for size-feedback loops.
issues_found.fix: Wrap setSize in requestAnimationFrame, or in startTransition. Or compare to previous size and skip if equal.
issues_found.verification: Build a self-feeding layout — no warning.

issues_found.title: Re-renders on equal sizes (sub-pixel jitter)
issues_found.explanation: ResizeObserver fires on any size change including sub-pixel changes from device-pixel-ratio rounding. setSize gets called with 100.001 then 100.000; both trigger re-renders of all consumers.
issues_found.root_cause: No equality check.
issues_found.fix: setSize(prev => prev.width === w && prev.height === h ? prev : { width: w, height: h }). Or round to integers if sub-pixel doesn't matter.
