issues_found.verification: Profile during resize — re-renders only on genuine size change.

issues_found.title: entries[0] assumption
issues_found.explanation: Observer callback receives an array. On rapid resizes the array can have multiple entries (batched). entries[0] is the first, not the latest. With a single observed element this is usually fine, but if the hook is reused with multiple observe calls, the assumption breaks.
issues_found.root_cause: Convention assumption.
issues_found.fix: Find the entry matching the observed target, or iterate and take the last.
issues_found.verification: Observe multiple elements (if extended) — sizes match the right element.

issues_found.title: Tuple return order is non-obvious
issues_found.explanation: [ref, size] requires destructuring with implicit position. An object return is friendlier and easier to extend.
issues_found.root_cause: Tuple cuteness over readability.
issues_found.fix: return { ref, size }.
issues_found.verification: Consumer code clearer.

issues_found.title: Initial 0,0 causes visible jump on first render
issues_found.explanation: On first render size is 0,0; after observer fires, size updates. Components computing layout from size flash at 0 then real.
issues_found.root_cause: Initial value disconnected from element.
issues_found.fix: On the first render after element attaches, read element.getBoundingClientRect() synchronously to seed.
issues_found.verification: No 0,0 flash on first paint.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using as const for the tuple — correct. Generic T extends HTMLElement — fine.
broader_pattern: Hooks that observe DOM must use callback refs (not useRef + useEffect) when the observed element lifecycle is independent of the hook's lifecycle. Equality-check before setState in event-driven callbacks. And any callback that fires often (resize, scroll, intersection) needs an rAF or transition wrapper to avoid feedback loops.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The callback-ref pattern is unfamiliar to most devs but is the correct tool whenever you need to react to a DOM element appearing/disappearing. The ResizeObserver-loop-limit warning is exactly the kind of bug that floods Sentry and gets ignored as noise but actually indicates a layout feedback loop. requestAnimationFrame batching for observer callbacks is hard-won — most devs learn it after debugging 'why is my page running at 20fps when nothing's animating.'

scenario: A save-on-blur form field that PATCHes the change to the server.
submitter_context: PR: 'EditableField saves on blur. Shows saved/dirty state.'
review_summary: Disabling the input on save causes the user to lose focus mid-edit (and blur fires again on enable). value vs initial comparison uses prop initial — stale if parent updates initial after a save. Multiple rapid blurs (tab through fields fast) issue overlapping saves — last write wins regardless of typing order. No error handling — failed save silently 'succeeds.' No conflict resolution if parent re-renders with stale initial.

issues_found.title: Disabling input mid-save throws away user input
issues_found.explanation: User types 'hello', blurs to tab. saving=true disables input. User clicks back to keep editing — can't, it's disabled. By the time save finishes (300ms later), the user has already pressed keys that went to body / next element. Lost input.
issues_found.root_cause: Disabling for visual feedback rather than read-only.
issues_found.fix: Don't disable; show a saving indicator. Or use readOnly during save (focusable, not editable) and re-enable immediately. Better: optimistic UI — show saved instantly, surface error if it fails.
issues_found.verification: Type, blur, click back during save — must continue editing seamlessly.

issues_found.title: initial prop becomes stale after save
issues_found.explanation: After save, parent likely refetches and passes new initial. But this component holds value in local state — useState ignores prop changes after mount. Now value === initial check compares to the original prop, not the latest. Editing back to the new initial fires a redundant save.
issues_found.root_cause: useState only reads initial once. Need to sync.
issues_found.fix: Track lastSaved separately. Compare value to lastSaved, not initial. Or use useEffect to sync initial → value when initial changes (with care).
issues_found.verification: Save, then parent updates initial, edit back — should not re-save.

issues_found.title: No request ordering or debounce
issues_found.explanation: User edits A, blurs (save A). Immediately edits B, blurs (save B). If save A is slower, it resolves AFTER save B but its update overwrites — last response wins, not last edit. Server now thinks the field is A even though user typed B last.
issues_found.root_cause: Stale response wins (review-003 sibling).
issues_found.fix: AbortController; abort prior save on new save. Or queue and only let the latest fire.
issues_found.verification: Slow first save, fast second — final server value matches last typed.

issues_found.title: No error handling — failed save looks successful
issues_found.explanation: onSave rejects → await throws → setSaving(false) never runs and the user sees the spinner forever. Or with try/finally added, the spinner disappears but no error surfaces — user thinks it saved.
issues_found.root_cause: Async no try/catch.
issues_found.fix: try/catch/finally. Show error state with retry. Revert value to lastSaved on failure (or keep dirty for user to fix).
issues_found.verification: Server 500 — error surfaced, edit retained.

issues_found.title: Blur fires on every blur event including programmatic
issues_found.explanation: Programmatic focus changes (e.g., from a modal opening) trigger blur. Saves fire on unrelated UI events. Also, blur in some browsers fires before related click handlers, leading to race with parent's onClick.
issues_found.root_cause: Treating blur as a reliable user-intent signal.
issues_found.fix: Track focused/dirty state explicitly; save on blur only if dirty AND blur wasn't to a related element.
issues_found.verification: Open a modal that doesn't take focus — must not save.

issues_found.title: 'Saving…' not accessible
issues_found.explanation: Status not in an aria-live region. SR users get no feedback on save state.
issues_found.root_cause: Visual feedback only.
issues_found.fix: {saving ? 'Saving…' : 'Saved'}
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces save state.

issues_that_arent_issues: Skipping save-if-unchanged is correct policy. async on event handler is fine.
broader_pattern: Edit-on-blur looks simple but it's a tiny database UI: needs optimistic update, request cancellation, error recovery, dirty tracking, and prop-vs-local-state sync. Reach for a mutation library (TanStack Mutation, React Hook Form with submit-on-blur). The general rule: any user-initiated server-write needs cancellation, idempotency, and explicit success/error UX.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The disabled-on-save UX bug is the senior catch — most reviewers see 'disabled when busy' as a feature, not the input-stealing bug it actually is. The prop-vs-local-state-sync issue is a perennial confusion ('how do I update state from props?') with no clean docs answer; seniors know the pattern (track explicitly). The blur-on-programmatic-focus issue is hard-won knowledge from 'why is my form auto-saving when nothing changed.'

scenario: Zustand store for shopping cart with derived totals.
submitter_context: PR: 'Cart store with add/remove. Total computed in store. Persisted to localStorage.'
review_summary: Stored total is computed redundantly in three places, and the multi-set updates produce inconsistent intermediate states. Total should be derived (selector), not stored — single source of truth. Plus setQty(id, 0) keeps an item in the cart with qty 0. No max-qty validation. Persist hydration race on SSR. And tax/shipping logic will land soon and need centralized derivation.

issues_found.title: Total can drift out of sync with items
issues_found.explanation: Total is computed in three places, manually. If anyone adds a new mutation (e.g., applyDiscount), they'll forget to update total. Over time total drifts. The bug is detected by a customer complaint: 'My cart says $40 but checkout charges $60.' Real-money bug.
issues_found.root_cause: Storing derived state instead of computing it.
issues_found.fix: Remove total from state. Expose a useCartTotal() selector: useCart(s => s.items.reduce(...)). Or use a getter via subscribeWithSelector.
issues_found.verification: Add a new mutation; total recomputes for free.

issues_found.title: Two-step set produces inconsistent snapshot
issues_found.explanation: Subscribers fire after the first set() with new items but old total. If any consumer renders both, they see an inconsistent UI for one frame: items show 3 entries but total reflects 2. Visible flicker.
issues_found.root_cause: Multiple set() calls when one would do.
issues_found.fix: Single set() call that updates both atomically. Better: only set items and derive total via selector.
issues_found.verification: Subscribe to both items and total — must always be consistent.

issues_found.title: qty=0 leaves a phantom line item
issues_found.explanation: User decrements qty to 0; the line still exists in state. Cart shows '0 × Shirt = $0' line. Confusing UX. Also bloats persisted state.
issues_found.root_cause: No invariant 'qty >= 1.'
issues_found.fix: setQty with qty <= 0 should remove the item.
issues_found.verification: Decrement to 0 — line must disappear.

issues_found.title: SSR hydration race + stale persisted state
issues_found.explanation: persist middleware re-hydrates from localStorage on client. During SSR, the store is empty. Server renders cart-empty UI; client hydrates, store rehydrates, re-renders with the actual cart — hydration mismatch + visible flash. Also: if the schema changes, old localStorage data may not parse.
issues_found.root_cause: Async hydration not coordinated with SSR.
issues_found.fix: Use skipHydration: true and rehydrate manually after mount inside a 'use client' boundary. Add a version key and migration function.
issues_found.verification: View source → empty cart; first paint matches; then cart hydrates.

issues_found.title: qty can be any number
issues_found.explanation: setQty(id, 999_999) succeeds. User-facing input can submit large numbers; client doesn't validate. Server might also not validate — risk of stock issues or pricing exploits.
issues_found.root_cause: No invariants in mutator.
issues_found.fix: Clamp qty to a reasonable max (e.g., 99) and reject negatives. Server-side validation is non-negotiable.
issues_found.verification: Try qty=999999 — clamped or rejected.

issues_found.title: Inconsistent money representation
issues_found.explanation: priceCents is integer cents — good practice. But total ends up being cents too without a clear name. UI rendering will need /100. Worth documenting.
issues_found.root_cause: Implicit unit.
issues_found.fix: Type Total = { cents: number } or rename to totalCents. Centralize formatting.
issues_found.verification: Search for /100 in UI; should be one place.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using create<>() with curried function for typing is correct Zustand idiom. persist middleware itself is fine.
broader_pattern: Stored vs derived state: derived state should be computed from stored state, never stored alongside. Storing derived = invariant violations. The rule: 'if I can compute it cheaply from other state, I don't store it.' For persisted stores on SSR: hydrate explicitly in a client boundary, version your schema, and migrate old shapes.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Storing derived state alongside source is the most common store bug, and 'remove total from state' is the kind of refactor juniors push back on ('but it's faster!'). The atomic-set bug is invisible to most reviewers but produces visible inconsistencies that show up in customer screenshots. The persist + SSR hydration race is a Next.js gotcha that costs hours the first time. And qty=0 phantom items is the kind of UX bug found only by exploratory testing.

scenario: Multi-step wizard form with step state and validation per step.
submitter_context: PR: 'Wizard form. Three steps. Validate before Next. Final submit POSTs everything.'
review_summary: Validation is silent (no message when blocked). Email isn't validated at all (step 0 only checks name). Going back to step 0 then forward loses no data — good — but going back and editing then jumping forward via Next button can submit with an empty required field on a previous step that user thought they'd filled. Submit has no error handling, no double-submit guard, no loading state. Browser back button moves user away — no leave-confirm. And per-step state is intermingled, not validated, not announced as steps to SR.

issues_found.title: Silent validation fail — user sees nothing
issues_found.explanation: Empty name on step 0 → click Next → button does nothing. No error, no focus to the failing field. Users repeatedly click 'Next', confused, eventually leaving the form. Conversion bug.
issues_found.root_cause: Validation as guard without user feedback.
issues_found.fix: Set error state per field; render aria-invalid + error message; focus first invalid field.
issues_found.verification: Click Next with empty name — error visible and field focused.

issues_found.title: Email collected but never validated
issues_found.explanation: Step 0 next() only checks data.name. User can submit with email='' or 'asdf'. Server validates, rejects, but UX wasted 3 steps to find out.
issues_found.root_cause: Per-step validation logic incomplete.
issues_found.fix: Schema-per-step (Zod). validate() against the schema before advancing.
issues_found.verification: Empty/invalid email blocks step 0.

issues_found.title: No double-submit, no loading, no error handling
issues_found.explanation: Same class as review-007: click Submit twice, two POSTs. Network failure leaves user with no feedback. No success state.
issues_found.root_cause: Mutation-as-fire-and-forget.
issues_found.fix: useTransition or useRef lock; try/catch; success state; error display.
issues_found.verification: Hammer Submit — one POST.

issues_found.title: User can navigate away mid-form, losing data
issues_found.explanation: Half-filled form, accidental back swipe on mobile or browser back, all state lost. No warning. Common cause of abandonment.
issues_found.root_cause: State only in memory.
issues_found.fix: beforeunload listener while dirty; persist to localStorage and offer to restore.
issues_found.verification: Fill, refresh — offered to restore.

issues_found.title: No step indicator + no announcement to SR
issues_found.explanation: User has no idea how many steps remain. SR doesn't announce 'step 2 of 3.' a11y + UX failure.
issues_found.root_cause: Visual progress not implemented.
issues_found.fix: Progress indicator with aria-current, aria-label='Step 2 of 3.' Use aria-live to announce step changes.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver — announces step.

issues_found.title: setData with stale closure on rapid changes
issues_found.explanation: Same as review-016: spread non-updater form, lost characters under rapid typing.
issues_found.root_cause: Spread without updater.
issues_found.fix: setData(prev => ({...prev, name: e.target.value})).
issues_found.verification: Type fast; no dropped chars.

issues_found.title: Label wrapping is good but explicit linking could be clearer
issues_found.explanation: Nesting input in label is technically associated, but explicit htmlFor + id is more SR-reliable.
issues_found.root_cause: Stylistic choice with marginal a11y benefit.
issues_found.fix: Add id + htmlFor.
issues_found.verification: SR announces both label and state.

issues_that_arent_issues: Single form data object is fine. Using {step === 0 && ...} pattern is fine. Submit handler being async is fine.
broader_pattern: Multi-step forms are an entire UX subsystem. Each step needs visible validation, focus management, a progress indicator, persistence across navigation, and aria-live announcements. The temptation to roll your own is strong; reach for react-hook-form + a wizard library or build the helpers carefully. Single-page wizards without history support hurt conversion measurably.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Spotting silent-validation as a conversion bug is product-eyes, not engineer-eyes — most reviewers say 'it works.' The localStorage draft + beforeunload combo is the only reason real users don't lose data on accidental refresh, but most apps skip it. Step-of-N aria-live announcement is a thing screen-reader users specifically request; sighted devs don't think of it.

scenario: A list of cards with staggered fade-in animation on scroll into view.
submitter_context: PR: 'Staggered fade-in for cards using IntersectionObserver + setTimeout delays.'
review_summary: Looks polished but has half a dozen issues. One observer per card (50 cards = 50 observers). No prefers-reduced-motion respect — a11y violation. setTimeout delay isn't cleared on unmount. Animation re-fires on every intersection (scroll out and back). And opacity-0 elements are still in tab order — keyboard users tab into invisible content. The 'staggered' effect requires correct index but you'd typically virtualize, breaking the staircase order.

issues_found.title: No reduced-motion respect
issues_found.explanation: Users with vestibular disorders or motion sensitivity set OS preferences. Forcing animation on them is an a11y violation (WCAG 2.3.3). 100-500ms staggered fades can trigger nausea in sensitive users.
issues_found.root_cause: Animations applied unconditionally.
issues_found.fix: Check prefers-reduced-motion media query; show content instantly if set. In Tailwind v4: motion-reduce:transition-none.
issues_found.verification: OS reduced motion on — cards appear instantly.

issues_found.title: Invisible cards are focusable — keyboard users land on nothing
issues_found.explanation: opacity-0 hides visually but content is still in the accessibility tree and focusable. Keyboard user tabs into a card they can't see, screen reader announces it.
issues_found.root_cause: Hiding visually only; not from accessibility.
issues_found.fix: Combine opacity with visibility: hidden (or display: none, but that breaks transitions). aria-hidden while invisible. Or use the upcoming inert attribute.
issues_found.verification: Tab through page — focus skips not-yet-visible cards (or they become visible to receive focus).

issues_found.title: 50 observers for 50 cards
issues_found.explanation: Each card creates its own IntersectionObserver — wasteful. Browsers handle them fine in small numbers but with hundreds, observer overhead becomes noticeable.
issues_found.root_cause: Per-component lifecycle in a list.
issues_found.fix: Single observer at the parent, mapping entry.target → callback. Or use a library (react-intersection-observer with sharedObserver option).
issues_found.verification: Memory profile — one observer instance, not N.

issues_found.title: Timeout not cleared on unmount
issues_found.explanation: Card unmounts during animation delay — setTimeout still fires, calls setState on unmounted component. React warns; in older React versions, leaks closures. With list virtualization (very common), this happens constantly.
issues_found.root_cause: Forgotten cleanup.
issues_found.fix: Capture timeout id; clear in cleanup.
issues_found.verification: Mount/unmount rapidly — no setState-on-unmounted warnings.

issues_found.title: Animation re-fires on scroll-out-and-back
issues_found.explanation: User scrolls past the card, then back up. Card animates again. Distracting and unintended for a 'reveal on first view' pattern.
issues_found.root_cause: Not unobserving after first reveal.
issues_found.fix: observer.unobserve(entry.target) once visible. Or check if visible state is already true.
issues_found.verification: Scroll past, scroll back — no re-animation.

issues_found.title: Staggered delay breaks with virtualization
issues_found.explanation: If list virtualizes (review-001 fix), index is the virtual index — not the visual stagger order. Cards in the viewport appear in a non-staircase pattern.
issues_found.root_cause: Stagger logic depends on stable enumeration.
issues_found.fix: Compute stagger from visual position (entry.boundingClientRect.top) or capped to viewport.
issues_found.verification: Virtualized list — stagger order matches visual order.

issues_found.title: Opacity transition is the right pick but on the right axis
issues_found.explanation: opacity is a compositor property — good. Pairing with transform-y for a 'rise' adds polish but stays GPU-only.
issues_found.root_cause: Subjective polish.
issues_found.fix: Combine with translate-y-2 → translate-y-0 if design wants it.
issues_found.verification: Visual check.

issues_that_arent_issues: IntersectionObserver is the right tool. Tailwind utilities for transition are fine.
broader_pattern: Reveal animations are an a11y minefield. Always respect prefers-reduced-motion, hide from focus when invisible, unobserve after first reveal, and share observers across instances. Animations that look great in marketing pages cost real users their well-being if not gated.
what_makes_this_review_senior: prefers-reduced-motion is the kind of thing senior reviewers internalize after a customer email saying 'your site makes me dizzy.' The 'invisible cards still focusable' bug is one of those that only blind users would file as a ticket — sighted devs literally cannot see it. The 'observer per card' note is judgment: technically fine for 50, terrible for 5000, you weigh against codebase scale.

scenario: An Editor component that lazy-loads CodeMirror.
submitter_context: PR: 'Lazy-load CodeMirror so the editor doesn't add to main bundle.'
review_summary: The 'lazy-loaded' Editor still imports CodeMirror eagerly at module scope. The imports of @codemirror/view, @codemirror/state, and four language modules pull the entire CM bundle into the parent module — defeating the lazy import. Plus all four language packs ship to every user even if they only edit JS.

issues_found.title: Eager imports defeat lazy boundary
issues_found.explanation: import { EditorView } from '@codemirror/view' at module scope pulls CodeMirror into this file's chunk. React.lazy only defers the chunk of CodeEditor.tsx; everything imported at the parent level is in the parent bundle. The 'lazy' optimization is undone by the imports above it.
issues_found.root_cause: Misunderstanding what lazy boundaries gate. They only gate what's inside the lazy module.
issues_found.fix: Move all CodeMirror imports into CodeEditor.tsx. The Editor wrapper should import nothing from @codemirror.
issues_found.verification: Bundle analyzer — main chunk has no @codemirror code; lazy chunk has it.

issues_found.title: All language packs loaded regardless of which is needed
issues_found.explanation: User editing JavaScript still downloads Rust, Python, SQL grammars (each ~50-200KB). The selection happens by string at runtime but all four are statically imported.
issues_found.root_cause: Static imports of a list of optional features.
issues_found.fix: Dynamic import per language inside CodeEditor based on language prop. Cache results so switching back doesn't re-fetch.
issues_found.verification: Open JS file — only @codemirror/lang-javascript loaded.

issues_found.title: No preload for likely-used editor
issues_found.explanation: User clicks a button that opens an editor — code starts downloading at that moment, showing a skeleton. If the editor is the page's main feature, this is 200-500ms of skeleton on first interaction.
issues_found.root_cause: Defer-only mindset.
issues_found.fix: On hover/focus of the button that opens the editor, prefetch the chunk: const promise = import('./CodeEditor'). For routes, use route-based preloading.
issues_found.verification: Hover button — chunk loads in background; click → instant.

issues_found.title: Skeleton has no h-96 reservation when content arrives
issues_found.explanation: animate-pulse + h-96 is fine for the skeleton, but when the editor loads, layout changes only if dimensions match. If CodeEditor renders at a different height, layout shifts (CLS).
issues_found.root_cause: Skeleton not size-matched to content.
issues_found.fix: Set explicit dimensions on CodeEditor that match the skeleton.
issues_found.verification: Lighthouse CLS score < 0.1 on this section.

issues_found.title: language prop is unconstrained
issues_found.explanation: language: string accepts anything; dynamic import('@codemirror/lang-' + language) is XSS-adjacent (path traversal via crafted prop value) and crashes on unknown languages.
issues_found.root_cause: Loose typing on a value used for module loading.
issues_found.fix: language: 'javascript' | 'python' | 'rust' | 'sql' as a union. Map to module loaders explicitly.
issues_found.verification: TypeScript rejects arbitrary strings.

issues_found.title: Lazy import failure crashes
issues_found.explanation: Chunk load can fail (network, deploy mid-session). Suspense rethrows; no boundary catches it.
issues_found.root_cause: Suspense without paired error boundary.
issues_found.fix: Wrap in ErrorBoundary with retry that re-imports.
issues_found.verification: Throttle network, simulate chunk failure — show retry UI.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using lazy + Suspense is the right shape. animate-pulse skeleton is fine.
broader_pattern: Code-splitting boundaries only split what's inside them. Imports at module scope of the parent are eager. The mental rule: 'every static import is a request to load that module before this one runs.' For optional features (language packs, charts, editors), prefer dynamic import() based on actual user intent. Pair lazy with preload-on-hover, sized skeletons, and error boundaries.
what_makes_this_review_senior: This is the bug that 'looks lazy but isn't.' Senior reviewers always check whether the import boundary actually contains the heavy thing. Per-language dynamic imports is a polish move that only happens when someone notices in the bundle analyzer that 80% of users never touch Rust files. Preload-on-hover is one of those tricks that turns 'snappy' into 'instant' and is invisible until you don't have it.

scenario: useAutoSave hook that saves form data to the server after a delay.
submitter_context: PR: 'useAutoSave saves data 2s after the last edit. Cancels if data changes again.'
review_summary: Three issues. save in deps means a fresh save function on each render re-arms the timer — if the parent re-renders for any reason (unrelated state change), the timer resets, never firing. Without that, data being an object causes every render to look like a change (referential inequality). And save fires-and-forgets — if it fails, user has no idea their data is unsaved.

issues_found.title: Unstable save callback resets timer every render
issues_found.explanation: Consumer almost always passes inline save: (d) => api.save(d). Each render = new reference, deps change, effect cleans up and re-arms. With a 2s timer and a render every 500ms (common when typing), the timer never fires. User thinks autosave is working; data never saved.
issues_found.root_cause: Including unstable callback in deps; consumers don't useCallback by reflex.
issues_found.fix: Store save in a ref; effect deps just [data, delayMs]. Effect reads saveRef.current at fire time.
issues_found.verification: Re-render parent unrelatedly while typing — autosave still fires.

issues_found.title: Object data triggers on every render
issues_found.explanation: If consumer passes data: { name, email } inline, that's a new object every render. Effect treats every render as a 'data changed' and re-arms. Combined with #1, double-broken.
issues_found.root_cause: Reference equality on object data.
issues_found.fix: Document that consumer must pass stable data (memoized). Or accept a serialization function and compare strings. Or deep-compare with use-deep-compare-effect.
issues_found.verification: Stable data + unrelated render — no timer reset.

issues_found.title: No error handling — silent failures
issues_found.explanation: save returns a promise. Hook doesn't await or handle errors. Network failure → save lost. User keeps editing, eventually closes tab, data gone.
issues_found.root_cause: Fire-and-forget mutation.
issues_found.fix: Track status; return { status, lastSavedAt, error }. Caller renders feedback (Saved 2s ago / Saving / Error).
issues_found.verification: Server returns 500 — caller can show 'unsaved changes' indicator.

issues_found.title: Save fires after unmount with stale data
issues_found.explanation: Timer hasn't fired yet; component unmounts. Cleanup clears timeout — but what if it just fired? The save is in flight. If user navigated away, the save might overwrite a fresh server state with stale data.
issues_found.root_cause: No save coordination across lifecycle.
issues_found.fix: AbortController; abort in-flight save on unmount. Consider a beforeunload save for unsaved data.
issues_found.verification: Save in flight, unmount — abort cleanly.

issues_found.title: Overlapping saves on rapid changes
issues_found.explanation: User edits, 2s timer fires, save starts (slow). User edits again, timer fires again at 2s — second save starts. Both in flight. If first finishes after second, server has stale data.
issues_found.root_cause: No 'one in flight at a time' policy.
issues_found.fix: Track in-flight; chain or abort prior. AbortController again.
issues_found.verification: Throttle to slow 3G; type fast — only last save wins on server.

issues_found.title: No way to read status
issues_found.explanation: Consumer can't render saved/dirty/saving state. They reinvent it around the hook.
issues_found.root_cause: Underspecified return type.
issues_found.fix: Return { status, lastSavedAt, error, save: () => Promise<void> } so consumer can manually save (Cmd+S).
issues_found.verification: UI shows 'Last saved 5s ago.'

issues_that_arent_issues: useEffect for debounced action is fine. setTimeout/clearTimeout is the right primitive.
broader_pattern: Hooks that take callback props need to think hard about callback stability. The two solutions: (a) require consumers to useCallback (rare in practice), (b) store callback in a ref. The latter is the senior pattern. Pair with explicit returned status — consumers always need to render save state and you save them rewriting it.
what_makes_this_review_senior: The 'unstable callback in deps' bug is the #1 source of broken-debouncers in production. Telling people to useCallback works in theory and fails in practice (devs forget). The ref pattern is the senior workaround. Returning a status object instead of void is the kind of API design lesson that comes from supporting your own hook used in 30 places — you'll thank yourself later.

scenario: A shared Button component used across the app.
submitter_context: PR: 'Button with size/variant variants. Built on top of native button.'
looks_reasonable_because: Variant API similar to many libraries
review_summary: Six issues hide in this 'simple' component. Missing type='button' default (submits forms by accident). loading state doesn't disable the button (double-click submits twice). Doesn't forward ref (no focus(), no popovers anchoring to it). No support for asChild / link rendering — consumers wrap with <a> and break button semantics. className override clobbers internal classes instead of merging properly. And missing aria-busy when loading.

issues_found.title: Missing type='button' default
issues_found.explanation: Default button type inside a form is 'submit.' Every Button rendered in a form submits the form on click. Open-menu buttons, secondary actions, all submit. Bug only triggers for buttons inside forms — so QA misses, customer support fields it.
issues_found.root_cause: HTML default-submit semantics.
issues_found.fix: type: 'button' | 'submit' = 'button' explicit default; require 'submit' to be explicit.
issues_found.verification: Put button in <form>, click — must not submit.

issues_found.title: loading=true doesn't disable
issues_found.explanation: User clicks Submit; button shows 'Loading...' but is still clickable. They click again; double-submit. Common cause of duplicate charges in checkout.
issues_found.root_cause: Visual state without behavioral guard.
issues_found.fix: disabled={disabled || loading}. Also aria-busy={loading}.
issues_found.verification: loading=true → onClick doesn't fire.

issues_found.title: Can't forward ref
issues_found.explanation: Component libraries (Radix, popovers, tooltips, focus management) need refs to anchor or focus. Without forwarded ref, consumers can't programmatically focus the button or attach a popover.
issues_found.root_cause: Function component without React.forwardRef. (Note: React 19 supports ref as prop on function components directly without forwardRef.)
issues_found.fix: React 19: accept ref as a regular prop. Older: wrap with forwardRef.
issues_found.verification: Consumer can <Button ref={r} />; r.current.focus() works.

issues_found.title: className override clobbers, not merges
issues_found.explanation: Consumer passes className='bg-green-600' — string concat results in 'bg-blue-600 bg-green-600' both present. Tailwind v4 in dev shows green (later wins), but with cn() / class-variance-authority deduplication is explicit. Some build setups can't predict which class wins.
issues_found.root_cause: Naive string concat for utility classes.
issues_found.fix: Use clsx + tailwind-merge (twMerge) so later utility classes properly override.
issues_found.verification: Override with className='bg-green-600' — green wins predictably.

issues_found.title: Replaces children with 'Loading...' string — layout shift + a11y loss
issues_found.explanation: Submit button text changes from 'Save' to 'Loading...' — button width changes, neighboring layout shifts. SR may not announce the change.
issues_found.root_cause: Replacing content rather than overlaying.
issues_found.fix: Render a spinner inside while keeping children but with opacity reduced. aria-busy='true'. Preserves width.
issues_found.verification: Click submit — button width doesn't change; spinner appears next to label.

issues_found.title: Consumers wrap with <a> and break semantics
issues_found.explanation: For navigation use cases consumers write . That nests a button in an anchor — invalid HTML, screen reader announces twice.
issues_found.root_cause: Component only supports rendering <button>.
issues_found.fix: Radix-style asChild prop, or render as 'a' when href provided. Use Slot pattern.
issues_found.verification: <Button href='/page'>Go</Button> renders <a> with button-like styling, no nested button.

issues_found.title: Icon position not configurable
issues_found.explanation: Always before children; some designs want after.
issues_found.root_cause: Single layout.
issues_found.fix: iconPosition?: 'start' | 'end' or accept leftIcon/rightIcon props.
issues_found.verification: Pass rightIcon — appears after children.

issues_that_arent_issues: Lookup tables for sizes/variants are idiomatic. Default size/variant is fine. Using <> for fragment is fine.
broader_pattern: Shared components are platform code: design for every consumer's worst-case use. Default to safe behaviors (type='button'), forward refs, support polymorphism (asChild), merge classes properly, and preserve layout under state changes. The rule of thumb: imagine 50 different teams using this — what will they all need?
what_makes_this_review_senior: The 'no type='button' default' bug ships in every fresh React project until someone is bitten by it. The asChild pattern is library-design wisdom from Radix; juniors don't reach for it because they haven't yet had to handle 'I want this button to be a link.' twMerge over naive concat is something only seniors flag because the bug is hard to predict (depends on Tailwind v4's order resolution) and they've seen it bite. The 'loading replaces children → layout shift' is product polish that earns 'wow that's smooth.'

scenario: Image uploader with client-side resize before upload.
submitter_context: PR: 'Resize images client-side before upload. Uses canvas. 80% JPEG quality.'
looks_reasonable_because: Canvas resize is the right approach
review_summary: Works for the happy path on small images. In production it breaks for: large images (mobile photos are 50MB+, canvas exceeds browser memory cap), iOS Safari (canvas size limits + EXIF rotation ignored = sideways photos), accidentally uploaded HEIC files (browsers can't decode), object URLs never revoked (memory leak per upload). Plus no size validation upfront, no dimension validation, no progress, and ratio>1 logic doesn't upscale (good) but doesn't preserve original (potentially bad for small images).

issues_found.title: Large photos exceed iOS Safari canvas memory cap
issues_found.explanation: iOS Safari caps canvas size at ~16M pixels (4096x4096). Mobile photos are commonly 12+ MP. Drawing the image to canvas exceeds cap; canvas silently fills with blank/black. Upload succeeds but image is broken. No error fires. Only reproduces on actual iOS devices, not desktop Safari.
issues_found.root_cause: Browser-specific canvas memory limits.
issues_found.fix: Pre-shrink with multiple scale-down passes if image >some threshold. Use createImageBitmap + resizeWidth/resizeHeight when available (handles this transparently). Or use a worker with OffscreenCanvas.
issues_found.verification: Test on iPhone with a 12MP photo — uploaded blob must not be blank.

issues_found.title: EXIF orientation ignored — sideways photos
issues_found.explanation: Most phone cameras store image data in landscape orientation and embed an EXIF orientation tag (rotate 90° / 180° / etc.). drawImage to canvas ignores EXIF — image appears sideways after upload. Users complain 'my profile photo is sideways.'
issues_found.root_cause: Canvas API doesn't honor EXIF.
issues_found.fix: Read EXIF orientation (e.g., exif-js or piexifjs) and apply rotation/flip before drawing. Or rely on createImageBitmap with imageOrientation: 'from-image' (well-supported in 2026).
issues_found.verification: Take portrait photo on phone, upload — must remain portrait.

issues_found.title: Object URLs leaked, never revoked
issues_found.explanation: URL.createObjectURL(file) + URL.createObjectURL(blob) each create blob URLs that hold the underlying memory until URL.revokeObjectURL is called or the document unloads. Upload a dozen photos in one session → tens of MB leaked. On mobile, browser tab gets killed.
issues_found.root_cause: Missing revoke.
issues_found.fix: URL.revokeObjectURL after img.onload (for original); when preview is replaced or component unmounts. Track preview URL in ref so cleanup can access it.
issues_found.verification: DevTools memory snapshot — blob URLs after upload should not retain images.

issues_found.title: HEIC files accepted but not decodable
issues_found.explanation: image/* matches HEIC on iOS, but most browsers can't decode HEIC. new Image() onload never fires. Promise hangs forever. User sees nothing happen, no error.
issues_found.root_cause: Trusting accept attribute as a decode guarantee.
issues_found.fix: Validate file.type explicitly against decodable formats (jpeg, png, webp). For HEIC, reject with a helpful message or use a HEIC→JPEG converter library.
issues_found.verification: Upload HEIC from iPhone — show error 'Convert to JPEG first.'

issues_found.title: 50MB images consume the browser
issues_found.explanation: User uploads RAW or massive image; before any resize, you read it into Image — full decoded buffer in memory. Page jank or crash. No upfront check.
issues_found.root_cause: Size validation only by file dialog (which doesn't enforce).
issues_found.fix: Check file.size > 10*1024*1024 → reject with 'Too large.' Reasonable upper bound depending on UX.
issues_found.verification: Try 50MB image — rejected with friendly error.

issues_found.title: img.onerror not handled
issues_found.explanation: Corrupt image, network drop on URL load, etc. Promise never resolves. Hung state.
issues_found.root_cause: Single-path async.
issues_found.fix: Promise wraps both onload and onerror; reject on error.
issues_found.verification: Upload corrupted file — show error promptly.

issues_found.title: Preview img alt is empty
issues_found.explanation: For a user-uploaded photo it's debatable but better to say 'Image preview' for screen readers.
issues_found.root_cause: Empty alt for decorative-treated content.
issues_found.fix: alt='Image preview'.
issues_found.verification: VoiceOver announces meaningfully.

issues_that_arent_issues: Using canvas for resize is reasonable. JPEG quality 0.8 is a fine default. setPreview pattern is fine.
broader_pattern: File upload code must consider: device limits (iOS canvas, memory), file format diversity (HEIC, WebP, AVIF), file metadata (EXIF orientation, ICC profiles), size validation (upfront and after decode), object URL lifecycle, and progress + error UX. The 'happy path' is desktop Chrome with a small JPEG; production is millions of mobile users with 12MP HEIC photos.
what_makes_this_review_senior: Knowing the iOS canvas memory cap is the single hardest catch — it requires either reading platform engineering posts or debugging a customer ticket where 'my profile picture is broken but only on iPhone.' EXIF orientation is the bug that costs every social app a week the first time it ships. createImageBitmap with imageOrientation:'from-image' is the modern fix that didn't exist a few years ago; staying current with platform APIs is part of being senior. Object URL leaks are invisible until you watch a heap profile during a long session — a senior remembers to add the cleanup the first time, not after the bug report.
